meta.description: Gauntlet-tier CLF-C02 questions: edge cases, dense distractors, deliberate service-confusion traps. Sudden-death mode.

questions.scenario: A startup is running every workload on a single EC2 instance in us-east-1a. Leadership keeps hearing the words 'reliability' and 'availability' in board meetings and wants to know what's missing.
questions.question: Which AWS Well-Architected pillar MOST directly addresses the ability to recover from infrastructure failures with minimum disruption?
questions.explanation:
The Reliability pillar is specifically about a workload's ability to perform its intended function correctly and consistently, including recovery from failures and scaling to meet demand. Multi-AZ design, fault isolation, and automated recovery all live here.

Performance Efficiency (A) is about using compute resources efficiently — right-sizing, selecting the right instance type, monitoring. Operational Excellence (B) covers running and monitoring systems, continuous improvement, and runbook quality — related but not the same as recovering from failure. Security (D) is confidentiality, integrity, and access controls. The trap: candidates often pick Operational Excellence because it sounds like 'keeping things running,' but recovery from infrastructure failure is squarely a Reliability concept.
questions.pattern: Six pillars of Well-Architected: Operational Excellence, Security, Reliability, Performance Efficiency, Cost Optimization, Sustainability. Key distinctions — Reliability = recovery + availability; Operational Excellence = run/monitor/improve processes; Performance Efficiency = right-sized resources. If the question mentions 'recover from failure,' the answer is almost always Reliability.

questions.scenario: A security engineer is rotating a database password stored in AWS Secrets Manager. The application retrieves the secret at runtime using an IAM role. The engineer wants the rotation to happen automatically every 30 days without application downtime.
questions.question: Which AWS service integration enables automatic rotation of the secret?
choices: AWS Systems Manager Parameter Store with a scheduled EventBridge rule
AWS Secrets Manager with a Lambda rotation function
AWS KMS with a custom key policy that expires every 30 days
AWS IAM with a credential report triggered monthly
questions.explanation:
Secrets Manager has native automatic rotation that invokes an AWS-managed or custom Lambda rotation function on a defined schedule. For supported databases (RDS, Redshift, DocumentDB), AWS provides prebuilt rotation Lambdas — you configure the schedule, Secrets Manager handles the rest.

Parameter Store (A) stores configuration and secrets but does NOT provide native rotation — you'd have to build the whole rotation pipeline yourself. KMS key policies (C) don't expire on a schedule, and KMS keys protect secrets but are not themselves rotated this way (KMS has its own annual key rotation that's unrelated). IAM credential reports (D) are audit artifacts, not rotation mechanisms. The trap: Parameter Store sounds like it should do rotation because it 'also stores secrets,' but only Secrets Manager has the rotation feature.
questions.pattern: Parameter Store vs. Secrets Manager: Parameter Store = config + secrets, free tier available, NO native rotation. Secrets Manager = secrets only, costs per secret + API call, HAS native rotation via Lambda. If the question mentions 'automatic rotation,' the answer is Secrets Manager.

references.title: Comparing Secrets Manager and Parameter Store

questions.scenario: A company's security team wants a single, consolidated view of findings across GuardDuty, Inspector, Macie, and IAM Access Analyzer for all accounts in their AWS Organization. They do not want to build custom aggregation.
questions.question: Which service provides this consolidated view natively?
questions.explanation:
AWS Security Hub is the aggregation layer specifically designed to ingest findings from GuardDuty, Inspector, Macie, IAM Access Analyzer, Firewall Manager, and many third-party tools, normalizing them into the AWS Security Finding Format (ASFF) and presenting them in a single dashboard across accounts and regions.

Trusted Advisor (A) gives best-practice recommendations across cost, performance, security, fault tolerance, and service limits — it is not a security-findings aggregator. Config (C) tracks resource configuration changes and compliance against rules, but does not centralize findings from GuardDuty/Inspector/Macie. CloudWatch (D) is metrics, logs, and alarms — not a security-finding aggregation service. The trap: Trusted Advisor has 'security' checks, but 'consolidated view of findings from GuardDuty/Inspector/Macie' is explicitly Security Hub's job.
questions.pattern: Security service map: GuardDuty = threat detection, Inspector = vulnerability scanning, Macie = S3 data classification/PII, IAM Access Analyzer = unintended access paths. Security Hub = aggregator for all of the above. Trusted Advisor = best-practice scorecard, not a security aggregator.

questions.scenario: A developer is confused about the AWS Shared Responsibility Model while configuring an Amazon RDS instance. They want to know exactly which of these tasks is AWS's responsibility, not theirs.
questions.question: Under the Shared Responsibility Model, which task is AWS solely responsible for on an Amazon RDS database?
choices: Applying OS-level patches to the underlying host
Choosing the database engine version to run
questions.explanation:
For RDS (a managed service), AWS patches the underlying OS and hypervisor — the customer does not have OS access. This is a key distinction from EC2, where OS patching is the customer's responsibility.

Choosing the engine version (B) is the customer's choice — AWS provides engines and versions, but the customer selects and (to some extent) controls upgrade timing. Parameter group configuration (C) is explicitly the customer's job — AWS provides defaults and the customer tunes. Encryption at rest (D) is enabled by the customer (AWS provides the mechanism via KMS, but the customer must choose to enable it at DB creation and manage keys). The trap: 'encrypting data at rest' sounds like AWS does it automatically, but the customer enables/configures it — AWS only supplies the tools.
questions.pattern: Shared Responsibility — AWS does 'security OF the cloud' (hardware, hypervisor, managed-service OS, physical security). Customer does 'security IN the cloud' (data, IAM, network config, guest OS on EC2, application security). For managed services (RDS, Lambda, DynamoDB), AWS handles more; for IaaS (EC2), customer handles more. Patching managed-service OS = AWS; patching EC2 guest OS = customer.

references.title: Amazon RDS and the Shared Responsibility Model

questions.scenario: A batch processing job runs once per week for about 4 hours and is fault-tolerant — if an instance is interrupted, the job can restart from a checkpoint. The team wants the lowest possible EC2 cost.
questions.question: Which EC2 purchasing option provides the LOWEST cost for this workload?
questions.explanation:
Spot Instances offer up to 90% discount off On-Demand pricing for interruptible, fault-tolerant workloads. A weekly 4-hour job with checkpointing is textbook Spot.

On-Demand (A) is the most expensive option — no commitment, no discount. Reserved Instances (B) require a 1- or 3-year commitment for a specific instance family in a specific region — but the workload only runs 4 hours per week, so you'd pay for 168 hours × 52 weeks = 8,736 hours/year of capacity while using only 208 hours. RIs are for steady-state workloads. Savings Plans (D) also require a committed hourly spend — same problem as RIs for bursty weekly workloads. Spot wins because you only pay when running, at a deep discount, and the workload tolerates interruption.
questions.pattern: EC2 purchasing-option decision tree: Fault-tolerant + interruptible → Spot (up to 90% off). Steady-state, predictable 24/7 → RI or Savings Plans (up to ~72%). Short-term, unpredictable, no interruption tolerance → On-Demand. Dedicated Hosts = licensing/compliance edge case. Trap: 'lowest cost' + 'fault-tolerant' = Spot almost every time.

questions.scenario: A solutions architect needs to migrate 500 TB of data from an on-premises data center to Amazon S3. The available network link is 100 Mbps, and the migration must complete within 30 days.
questions.question: Which AWS service is MOST appropriate?
choices: AWS DataSync over the existing network link
AWS Direct Connect with a 1 Gbps port
questions.explanation:
At 100 Mbps, transferring 500 TB takes roughly 500 × 1024 × 1024 × 8 / 100 seconds ≈ 485 days — well over the 30-day window. Snowball Edge is a physical appliance shipped to the customer, loaded with data, and returned to AWS; 500 TB fits within a few Snowball Edge devices and arrives within days.

DataSync (A) is network-based — same 100 Mbps bottleneck, won't fit. Direct Connect at 1 Gbps (C) would take ~48 days of continuous transfer AND provisioning Direct Connect itself typically takes weeks — won't meet the deadline. Storage Gateway (D) is for ongoing hybrid access patterns, not bulk one-time migration. The trap: Direct Connect sounds faster, but provisioning time plus math makes it fail the 30-day constraint.
questions.pattern: Migration sizing rule of thumb: run the math. At 100 Mbps ≈ 1 TB/day (generous). At 1 Gbps ≈ 10 TB/day. If dataset ÷ throughput > deadline, you need Snow Family. Snowball Edge holds up to ~80 TB usable per device; Snowmobile (now discontinued for new orders) for exabyte-scale. DataSync = online transfer optimization; Snow = offline physical shipping.

questions.scenario: A developer needs to decouple a web front-end from a back-end processing tier. The front-end will send thousands of messages per second that must be processed in order per user session, with exactly-once processing semantics.
questions.question: Which service should they use?
questions.explanation:
SQS FIFO queues provide ordered delivery within a message group (using MessageGroupId — perfect for 'in order per user session') and exactly-once processing (deduplication within a 5-minute window). FIFO queues support up to 3,000 messages/second with batching or 300 without — sufficient for 'thousands per second.'

SQS Standard (A) provides at-least-once delivery and best-effort ordering only — messages can arrive out of order and be duplicated. SNS Standard (C) is pub/sub and is also at-least-once, not ordered, and not exactly-once. EventBridge (D) is an event bus for routing/filtering events between services — not optimized for per-session ordering with exactly-once semantics. The trap: SQS Standard scales to near-unlimited throughput, so it looks attractive, but it fails the 'in order' and 'exactly-once' requirements.
questions.pattern: Messaging triad — SQS = queue (point-to-point, pull), SNS = pub/sub (fan-out, push), EventBridge = event bus (rules + routing). Standard vs FIFO — FIFO gives ordering + exactly-once but lower throughput. Keywords: 'order' + 'exactly-once' ⇒ FIFO. 'Fan-out to multiple subscribers' ⇒ SNS. 'Route events between SaaS/AWS services with filtering' ⇒ EventBridge.

questions.scenario: A company runs a legacy Windows file share on-premises and wants to migrate to a fully managed AWS service that preserves SMB protocol compatibility, NTFS permissions, and Active Directory integration.
questions.question: Which service BEST fits this use case?
choices: Amazon FSx for Windows File Server
questions.explanation:
FSx for Windows File Server is a managed Windows-native file system that natively speaks SMB, enforces NTFS ACLs, and integrates with AWS Managed Microsoft AD or self-managed AD. It is the drop-in replacement for Windows file shares.

EFS (A) is NFS-only — works for Linux, but not Windows SMB clients or NTFS ACLs. S3 + File Gateway (C) presents SMB/NFS to clients but is a hybrid gateway backed by S3 object storage — it does not preserve NTFS ACLs the way a native Windows file system does, and has different performance characteristics. FSx for Lustre (D) is for high-performance computing workloads (ML training, genomics) — not for general-purpose Windows file shares. The trap: File Gateway speaks SMB, but 'fully managed file system with NTFS + AD' is FSx for Windows.
questions.pattern: Storage-service decision matrix — Block = EBS (single-AZ, attached to EC2) or EBS Multi-Attach. File = EFS (Linux/NFS), FSx for Windows (Windows/SMB + AD), FSx for Lustre (HPC), FSx for NetApp ONTAP (hybrid multi-protocol), FSx for OpenZFS. Object = S3. Hybrid = Storage Gateway (File/Volume/Tape). If the question says 'Windows + SMB + AD,' the answer is FSx for Windows File Server.

references.title: Amazon FSx for Windows File Server

questions.scenario: A company needs to run a container-based microservice but does not want to manage any servers or clusters. They want per-second billing and automatic scaling based on request volume.
questions.question: Which compute option matches these requirements?
questions.explanation:
AWS Fargate is the serverless compute engine for containers — it works with both ECS and EKS. No EC2 instances to manage, no cluster capacity planning. Billing is per-second based on vCPU and memory allocated to the task. Fargate scales automatically as tasks are launched.

ECS on EC2 (A) and EKS on EC2 (B) both require the customer to manage the EC2 cluster — capacity, scaling, patching. That's explicitly what the question rules out. EC2 Auto Scaling (D) is for raw EC2 instances, not containers, and still requires server management. The trap: candidates confuse 'ECS' and 'Fargate' — ECS is the orchestrator, Fargate is a compute-launch-type for ECS/EKS. 'Container + no servers' = Fargate.
questions.pattern: Container options ladder — ECS on EC2 (you manage cluster), EKS on EC2 (Kubernetes, you manage cluster), Fargate (serverless, AWS manages everything). Trigger phrase: 'no servers to manage' or 'serverless containers' ⇒ Fargate. Kubernetes-specific features mentioned ⇒ EKS. Simpler orchestration ⇒ ECS.

questions.scenario: A company has 25 AWS accounts under an AWS Organization. The finance team wants a single consolidated bill, volume pricing discounts applied across all accounts, and the ability to move an account between organizational units without losing its billing history.
questions.question: Which feature combination is correct?
choices: Consolidated billing only — it provides a single bill and volume discounts automatically across all accounts in an organization
Consolidated billing + AWS Cost Explorer — billing aggregates across accounts and Cost Explorer provides per-account breakdowns
AWS Organizations with all features enabled + SCPs — SCPs handle billing aggregation
AWS Control Tower — it is required to enable consolidated billing
questions.explanation:
Consolidated billing is an Organizations feature — one payer account receives a single bill that aggregates all member-account usage, which qualifies the whole organization for volume tiers (e.g., S3, CloudFront data transfer). Cost Explorer lets finance drill into spend by account, service, tag, etc.

Option A says consolidated billing works 'only' — partially true, but omits Cost Explorer, which is the standard tool for per-account breakdowns. Option C conflates SCPs (access control) with billing — SCPs don't handle billing. Option D is wrong — Control Tower is a governance/landing-zone service, not a prerequisite for consolidated billing; any AWS Organization supports consolidated billing. The trap: many candidates assume Control Tower is required; it's not.
questions.pattern: Organizations feature map: consolidated billing (always on with Organizations), SCPs (access control, requires 'all features'), tag policies, backup policies, RCPs, AI service opt-out policies. Control Tower = opinionated multi-account setup on top of Organizations — optional. Cost Explorer = per-account/service spend breakdown. Volume discounts apply naturally across consolidated billing.

questions.scenario: A startup's engineering manager is reviewing AWS Support plans. They need 24/7 phone support, a Technical Account Manager (TAM), and guidance on well-architected reviews for production workloads.
questions.question: Which support plan meets ALL of these requirements?
questions.explanation:
Only Enterprise Support (and Enterprise On-Ramp, to a limited degree) includes a dedicated TAM. Enterprise Support also includes 24/7 phone/chat/email, well-architected reviews, operational reviews, and concierge billing assistance.

Basic (A) includes only documentation and community forums — no phone support, no TAM. Developer (B) is business-hours email access — no phone, no TAM. Business (C) includes 24/7 phone/chat/email but does NOT include a full TAM (Business has 'access to' some proactive guidance via the AWS Trusted Advisor API, but not a dedicated TAM). Enterprise On-Ramp includes a pool of TAMs (not dedicated). The trap: Business Support has 24/7 phone — tempting — but the TAM requirement eliminates everything below Enterprise.
questions.pattern: Support plan ladder (memorize features that appear on exam): Basic (free, docs/forums), Developer (business hours email, $29+), Business (24/7 phone, Trusted Advisor full, $100+), Enterprise On-Ramp (TAM pool, $5,500+), Enterprise (dedicated TAM, well-architected reviews, 15-min critical response, $15,000+). Trigger 'dedicated TAM' ⇒ Enterprise.

questions.scenario: An application serves static website assets (HTML, CSS, JS, images) to users globally. The team wants minimum latency, lowest possible cost, and protection against common web exploits like SQL injection.
questions.question: Which combination is MOST appropriate?
choices: Amazon S3 + Amazon CloudFront + AWS WAF
Amazon EC2 behind an Application Load Balancer + AWS WAF
Amazon S3 + Amazon API Gateway + AWS Shield
questions.explanation:
S3 hosts static assets cheaply. CloudFront caches at 600+ edge locations globally, reducing latency and origin load (and often costing less than serving from S3 directly due to lower data transfer pricing). WAF attaches to CloudFront and blocks SQL injection, XSS, and other OWASP-top-10 attacks at the edge.

EC2 + ALB (B) is expensive and overkill for static content — you're paying for compute 24/7 to serve files S3 serves for cents. S3 + API Gateway (C) misuses API Gateway (which is for REST/HTTP APIs, not static asset delivery) — and Shield Standard is included free on all AWS accounts but does NOT protect against SQL injection (Shield = DDoS, WAF = application-layer attacks). Lightsail (D) is a simplified hosting bundle but no edge caching or WAF. The trap: Shield vs WAF — Shield = DDoS (network/transport), WAF = application-layer exploits (SQL injection, XSS).
questions.pattern: Static site pattern: S3 (origin) → CloudFront (edge cache + HTTPS) → WAF (app-layer filtering) → Shield (DDoS, included). Global DNS = Route 53. For dynamic APIs, swap S3 for API Gateway/Lambda or ALB+ECS. Shield Standard is automatic + free; Shield Advanced = $3,000/month for large-scale DDoS response team.

references.title: Hosting a static website on Amazon S3

questions.scenario: An IAM user needs to be granted temporary access to an S3 bucket in another AWS account. The access should expire after 1 hour and should NOT require creating a new IAM user in the target account.
questions.question: What is the MOST appropriate mechanism?
choices: Create an IAM user in the target account with a 1-hour password expiration
Use AWS STS to assume an IAM role in the target account
Share the root user credentials of the target account
Generate a long-lived IAM access key and rotate manually after 1 hour
questions.explanation:
Cross-account access with temporary credentials is the textbook use case for IAM roles and AWS STS (Security Token Service). The target account creates a role with a trust policy allowing the source account to assume it; the source user calls sts:AssumeRole to receive temporary credentials (default 1 hour, configurable up to 12 hours).

Option A creates a permanent user — violates 'no new user in target account' and uses long-lived credentials. Option C is a catastrophic security violation — root credentials should never be shared or even used routinely. Option D uses long-lived access keys with manual rotation — fragile, error-prone, and not temporary in the programmatic sense (STS issues credentials that actually expire). The trap: password expiration (option A) and manual rotation (option D) sound 'temporary,' but they're workarounds; STS assume-role is the native mechanism.
questions.pattern: Cross-account access pattern: role in target account with trust policy + sts:AssumeRole from source. Benefits: no long-lived keys, automatic expiration, auditable via CloudTrail. Related concept: IAM Identity Center (formerly SSO) for human users, resource-based policies (like S3 bucket policies) for resource-level sharing. Never share root credentials — ever.

questions.scenario: A company runs a PostgreSQL database with unpredictable usage — some days it's idle, other days it's hit with sudden large workloads. They want a managed database that automatically scales capacity and pauses when idle to save cost.
questions.question: Which database service is the BEST fit?
choices: Amazon RDS for PostgreSQL with a read replica
questions.explanation:
Aurora Serverless v2 scales capacity up and down in fine-grained ACU (Aurora Capacity Unit) increments based on load, and can scale to zero when idle (when configured). It's PostgreSQL-compatible, so existing Postgres code works. This is precisely the bursty + idle pattern Aurora Serverless targets.

RDS single instance (A) has fixed capacity — doesn't scale automatically, doesn't pause. RDS with a read replica (B) offloads reads but still has fixed primary capacity. DynamoDB on-demand (D) is a NoSQL key-value/document store — not PostgreSQL-compatible, and the application presumably uses relational SQL. The trap: DynamoDB has the right billing model (on-demand), but wrong data model (NoSQL, not PostgreSQL).
questions.pattern: Relational options on AWS — RDS (provisioned, MySQL/Postgres/MariaDB/Oracle/SQL Server/Db2), Aurora (provisioned, MySQL/Postgres compatible, AWS-engineered, faster + cheaper than RDS for same workload), Aurora Serverless v2 (auto-scale capacity). NoSQL — DynamoDB (key-value/document), DocumentDB (MongoDB-compatible), Keyspaces (Cassandra), Neptune (graph). 'Bursty Postgres' ⇒ Aurora Serverless v2.

questions.scenario: A company's new workload has peaks of 10x normal traffic for a few hours each day, then drops back down. They used to provision for peak on-premises, leading to 80% idle hardware off-peak.
questions.question: Which cloud concept MOST directly resolves this inefficiency?
questions.explanation:
Elasticity is the ability to scale resources out and in to match demand — the cloud capability that eliminates the need to provision for peak. You pay for what you use, scale up during peaks, scale down off-peak.

High availability (A) is about uptime — keeping the system available despite failures. Fault tolerance (C) is about continuing to operate during component failure (a superset-ish concept). Global reach (D) is the ability to deploy in multiple regions worldwide. All are legitimate cloud benefits, but the specific 10x-then-drops pattern is answered by elasticity. The trap: 'scalability' and 'elasticity' are often used interchangeably in everyday speech, but on the exam 'elasticity' specifically means scale in both directions in response to demand.
questions.pattern: Cloud concepts vocabulary — Elasticity = scale out/in dynamically with demand. Scalability = ability to handle growth (could be vertical/horizontal, not necessarily automatic). High availability = uptime (typically via multi-AZ). Fault tolerance = continues operating despite failure (typically via redundancy + multi-region). Agility = speed of provisioning. Durability = data not lost (S3 = 11 nines).

questions.scenario: An application must invoke custom business logic in response to every object upload to an S3 bucket. The logic runs in milliseconds and must scale automatically with upload volume. The team does not want to manage servers.
questions.question: Which service is MOST appropriate for the compute tier?
choices: Amazon EC2 Auto Scaling behind an Application Load Balancer
AWS Lambda triggered by S3 event notifications
Amazon ECS on EC2 with step scaling
AWS Batch on a managed compute environment
questions.explanation:
Lambda is AWS's serverless compute — functions run in response to events, scale automatically from zero to thousands of concurrent executions, bill per millisecond, and require no server management. S3 has native event notifications that invoke Lambda on object creation.

EC2 Auto Scaling + ALB (A) requires managing instances, scaling policies, and an ALB — overkill and not aligned with 'no servers.' ECS on EC2 (C) also requires managing a cluster. Batch (D) is for long-running batch jobs (hours to days) — not millisecond event processing. The trap: Batch sounds like the word 'batch processing' but AWS Batch is specifically for queued, long-running compute jobs, not per-event execution.
questions.pattern: Serverless compute triggers: S3 event → Lambda, DynamoDB Stream → Lambda, API Gateway request → Lambda, EventBridge rule → Lambda, SNS/SQS → Lambda. Lambda limits: 15-min max runtime, 10 GB memory max, ephemeral /tmp up to 10 GB. Longer workloads ⇒ Fargate tasks, ECS, or Batch.

questions.scenario: A company's AWS bill jumped 40% unexpectedly last month. Finance wants to identify which service, account, and tag drove the increase, and to set up automated alerts when spend exceeds a threshold.
questions.question: Which two services together BEST address these needs? (Choose the option listing both.)
choices: AWS Trusted Advisor and AWS CloudTrail
AWS Cost Explorer and AWS Budgets
AWS Cost and Usage Report (CUR) and AWS Control Tower
questions.explanation:
Cost Explorer provides visual spend analysis with filters by service, account, tag, region, etc., including anomaly detection. AWS Budgets lets you define thresholds and send alerts (via SNS/email) when spend is forecast to exceed — or has exceeded — those thresholds. Together, they cover 'what drove the increase' and 'alert me next time.'

Trusted Advisor (A) gives cost-optimization recommendations but does not produce per-service/tag spend analytics; CloudTrail is API audit logging. Config (C) tracks resource configuration compliance; CloudWatch is metrics/logs — neither is billing-specific. CUR (D) is a granular billing data export (useful for deep analysis in Athena/QuickSight) but doesn't alert on its own; Control Tower is for landing zones, not billing analysis. The trap: CUR is tempting because it's 'cost' in the name, but it's a raw data feed, not an analysis/alerting tool.
questions.pattern: Cost-management toolkit: Cost Explorer (visual analytics, forecasts, anomaly detection), AWS Budgets (threshold-based alerts: cost, usage, RI coverage), Cost & Usage Report (raw CSV/Parquet feed for BI tools), Savings Plans/RI recommendations (inside Cost Explorer), AWS Billing Conductor (chargeback to business units), Compute Optimizer (right-sizing recommendations).

questions.scenario: A developer finds an S3 bucket that accidentally allows public READ access. They need to prevent any future S3 bucket in the account from being made public, regardless of individual bucket policies.
questions.question: Which feature should they enable?
choices: S3 Block Public Access at the account level
AWS WAF rule blocking public IPs
questions.explanation:
S3 Block Public Access can be enabled at the account level (as well as per-bucket) and, when enabled, overrides any bucket policy or ACL that would grant public access. This is the account-wide guardrail for preventing accidental public exposure.

Object Lock (A) prevents objects from being deleted or overwritten — compliance/WORM feature, unrelated to public access. WAF (C) filters HTTP requests to CloudFront/ALB/API Gateway — not S3 bucket-policy-level controls. Versioning (D) keeps object history — helps with recovery, not public access. The trap: candidates sometimes pick Object Lock because it sounds like 'locking down,' but it's about data immutability, not access.
questions.pattern: S3 security layers: bucket policy (resource policy), ACLs (legacy, mostly disabled), IAM policies (identity policies), Block Public Access (account-wide and per-bucket override), pre-signed URLs (time-limited access), VPC endpoints (private network access). Always enable Block Public Access at account level unless you have an explicit public-content use case.

references.title: Blocking public access to your Amazon S3 storage

questions.scenario: A gaming company needs a database that handles millions of user profile reads per second with single-digit millisecond latency globally. The data model is simple key-value lookups.
questions.question: Which database service is the BEST fit?
choices: Amazon RDS for MySQL with read replicas in multiple regions
Amazon Aurora MySQL with cross-region read replicas
questions.explanation:
DynamoDB is a NoSQL key-value store designed for millisecond latency at any scale. Global tables replicate data across regions with multi-active (read/write) semantics, so users worldwide get local-region latency. Perfectly matches 'millions of reads per second, globally, single-digit ms, key-value.'

RDS MySQL (A) and Aurora MySQL (D) are relational — great for complex queries, but not optimized for millions of simple key-value reads per second; cross-region replicas introduce replication lag and read-only secondaries. Redshift (C) is a data warehouse for analytical OLAP queries, not low-latency OLTP key-value lookups — wildly wrong service for this workload.
questions.pattern: Database selection by access pattern: OLTP relational queries ⇒ RDS/Aurora. High-throughput simple key-value ⇒ DynamoDB. Analytical columnar queries ⇒ Redshift. In-memory caching ⇒ ElastiCache (Redis/Memcached) or DynamoDB Accelerator (DAX). Multi-region active-active ⇒ DynamoDB Global Tables or Aurora Global Database (for SQL). Gaming leaderboards / session state ⇒ DynamoDB or ElastiCache.

questions.scenario: A team is debating whether to deploy across multiple Availability Zones or multiple Regions. They want to understand the difference in disaster recovery capability.
questions.question: Which statement is TRUE about AZs and Regions?
choices: AZs within a Region are in the same data center building, so multi-AZ protects against hardware failure but not disasters affecting the Region
AZs within a Region are physically separated data centers connected by low-latency links, and Regions are fully independent geographic areas providing protection against Region-wide disasters
All AWS services are automatically replicated across all Regions by default
Multi-Region deployment is required for high availability; multi-AZ alone is not considered highly available
questions.explanation:
AZs are distinct data centers (one or more) physically separated within a metropolitan area but interconnected by low-latency, high-bandwidth private fiber. Regions are independent geographic areas (typically 100+ miles apart) — a Region-wide disaster (rare) is mitigated by multi-Region.

Option A is wrong — AZs are physically separated, not in the same building. Option C is wrong — services are NOT automatically multi-Region; replication is opt-in (e.g., S3 Cross-Region Replication, DynamoDB Global Tables, Aurora Global Database). Option D is wrong — multi-AZ is the standard baseline for high availability (RDS Multi-AZ, Multi-AZ Auto Scaling Groups are 'highly available' designations); multi-Region is for DR, not a requirement for HA. The trap: option D sounds rigorous, but AWS's own definitions consider multi-AZ to qualify as HA.
questions.pattern: AWS global structure: Region (geographic area, 100+ miles between regions) → AZ (distinct data centers within region, typically <100 km apart, <10 ms inter-AZ latency) → Edge Location (CloudFront, hundreds globally). Availability patterns: single AZ = not HA. Multi-AZ in one Region = HA baseline. Multi-Region = DR + global presence. Choose based on RTO/RPO and latency requirements.

questions.scenario: An auditor requests a complete record of every API call made by every user and service in an AWS account for the past 90 days, including parameters and source IP addresses.
questions.question: Which AWS service provides this?
questions.explanation:
CloudTrail records every AWS API call (who, when, what action, what parameters, source IP, success/failure) across management events and, optionally, data events. This is the audit trail for AWS activity and is the go-to answer for 'who did what in my AWS account.' CloudTrail keeps 90 days of management events free; longer retention requires an S3 trail.

CloudWatch Logs (A) stores application and system logs (and can receive CloudTrail logs) but is not the native recorder of AWS API calls. Config (C) records resource configuration state changes over time (e.g., 'this security group was modified at this time') but not every API call — it's adjacent but not identical. VPC Flow Logs (D) captures network traffic metadata (src/dst IPs, ports, accept/reject), not API calls. The trap: Config vs CloudTrail — Config = resource state snapshots + changes, CloudTrail = API call audit log.
questions.pattern: Audit/observability triad: CloudTrail = WHO did WHAT via API (source IP, parameters). Config = resource CONFIGURATION STATE over time + compliance rules. CloudWatch = METRICS + LOGS + ALARMS + dashboards (health + performance). VPC Flow Logs = network traffic metadata. Guardrails on top: GuardDuty analyzes CloudTrail + VPC Flow Logs + DNS for threats.

questions.scenario: A company runs a web application on EC2 instances in a private subnet. The instances need to download OS patches and software updates from the internet without being exposed to inbound internet traffic.
questions.question: Which architecture is correct?
choices: Attach a public Elastic IP to each EC2 instance and allow outbound-only in the security group
Route traffic through a NAT Gateway in a public subnet
Attach an Internet Gateway to the private subnet's route table
Use a VPN connection to a software-update server
questions.explanation:
A NAT Gateway sits in a public subnet and allows instances in private subnets to initiate outbound connections to the internet (for patches, updates, API calls) while blocking unsolicited inbound connections. This is the canonical 'private subnet with outbound internet' pattern.

Option A would expose instances with public IPs — defeats the 'private subnet' goal. Option C is a misunderstanding — an Internet Gateway is attached to a VPC, not a subnet, and merely 'attaching IGW to a private subnet's route table' would make the subnet public (it would no longer be private by definition). Option D (VPN) is over-engineered for fetching public software updates. The trap: option A sounds plausible if you think 'outbound-only' equals private, but a public IP + IGW route means the instance can also be reached from the internet.
questions.pattern: VPC outbound patterns: public subnet = route 0.0.0.0/0 → IGW. Private subnet with internet = route 0.0.0.0/0 → NAT Gateway (in public subnet). NAT Gateway is managed/HA within an AZ (deploy one per AZ for AZ-level HA). NAT Instance is the self-managed legacy alternative. Egress-only Internet Gateway is the IPv6 equivalent of a NAT Gateway. For private access to AWS services without internet: VPC endpoints (Gateway for S3/DynamoDB, Interface for others).

questions.scenario: An application stores user-uploaded images in S3. 95% of accesses happen in the first 30 days. After 90 days, data is accessed at most once a year but must be retrievable within minutes. After 3 years, data must be retained for compliance but can be retrieved within 12 hours.
questions.question: Which S3 lifecycle configuration minimizes cost while meeting retrieval-time requirements?
choices: Standard → Standard-IA at day 30 → Glacier Instant Retrieval at day 90 → Glacier Deep Archive at day 1095
Standard → Glacier Flexible Retrieval at day 30 → Glacier Deep Archive at day 90
Standard → One Zone-IA at day 30 → Glacier Deep Archive at day 90
Standard → Intelligent-Tiering at day 30 — no further transitions needed
questions.explanation:
Option A aligns to the access pattern: Standard (0-30 days, hot reads), Standard-IA (30-90 days, lower cost for infrequent access with millisecond retrieval), Glacier Instant Retrieval (90 days-3 years, 'at most once a year, retrievable in minutes' = GIR's millisecond retrieval at archive prices), Glacier Deep Archive (3 years+, 12-hour retrieval tolerable).

Option B skips to Glacier Flexible at day 30 — Flexible Retrieval needs minutes-to-hours for standard retrieval, so 'retrievable within minutes' for year-1 data would require expedited retrieval; also, the 30-90 day window still sees some access, and Standard-IA is cheaper than Glacier-tier for that pattern. Option C puts data in One Zone-IA (loss of AZ = loss of data) — bad for user uploads, and skips Glacier Instant Retrieval despite the 'retrievable in minutes' constraint. Option D uses Intelligent-Tiering but doesn't move to Deep Archive — Intelligent-Tiering has archive tiers, but the question specifies deterministic tiering over time. The trap: Glacier Flexible Retrieval vs Instant Retrieval — IR = millisecond, FR = minutes (expedited) / hours (standard), DA = hours.
questions.pattern: S3 storage classes by retrieval latency + cost: Standard (ms, highest $), Standard-IA (ms, lower $ + retrieval fee), Intelligent-Tiering (auto), One Zone-IA (ms, single AZ, cheapest non-archive), Glacier Instant Retrieval (ms, archive $), Glacier Flexible Retrieval (min-hours), Glacier Deep Archive (12-48 hours, cheapest). Rule: 'retrievable in minutes but accessed rarely' ⇒ Glacier Instant Retrieval.

questions.scenario: A development team argues about which AWS Well-Architected pillar to prioritize. The CFO insists on reducing waste and avoiding unused resources. The CTO wants to ensure the system keeps running through failures. The security officer wants stronger access controls.
questions.question: Which pillar BEST addresses the CFO's concern?
questions.explanation:
Cost Optimization is the pillar about delivering business value at the lowest price point — right-sizing, eliminating unused resources, using managed services, matching supply with demand. 'Reducing waste and avoiding unused resources' is its language.

Performance Efficiency (A) is about using compute resources efficiently — adjacent but focused on technical fit, not spend. Operational Excellence (C) covers running and monitoring. Sustainability (D) is environmental impact (energy efficiency, for planet, not just budget). The trap: Sustainability overlaps with waste reduction, but 'avoiding unused resources' + CFO framing is Cost Optimization.
questions.pattern: Well-Architected pillar quick-match: Operational Excellence = run/monitor/improve. Security = protect data/systems. Reliability = recover + availability. Performance Efficiency = right-sized resources. Cost Optimization = maximize value, minimize spend. Sustainability = minimize environmental impact. Keywords: 'waste'/'unused resources'/'budget' ⇒ Cost Optimization. 'planet'/'carbon'/'energy' ⇒ Sustainability.

questions.scenario: A company wants to deploy an application with components that can fail independently without cascading. They want a principle to guide their architecture.
questions.question: Which AWS design principle BEST fits this requirement?
choices: Loose coupling via message queues or event-driven communication
Monolithic deployment to minimize surface area
Colocating all tiers on a single instance
questions.explanation:
Loose coupling is a foundational cloud principle: components communicate through well-defined interfaces (SQS queues, SNS topics, EventBridge, APIs) so failure or slowness in one doesn't propagate. Classic pattern: web tier writes to SQS, back-end processes at its own pace; either side can fail without taking the other down.

Vertical scaling (A) makes a single server bigger — opposite of the distributed pattern. Monolithic deployment (C) tightly couples components. Colocation (D) is maximum coupling. The trap: vertical scaling sounds 'more resilient' because one big server feels sturdy, but scaling up doesn't prevent cascading failures — it just concentrates risk.
questions.pattern: Cloud design principles: design for failure, loose coupling (queues/topics between tiers), horizontal scaling (scale out, not up), stateless services (state in DynamoDB/Redis/S3, not on instance), infrastructure as code, automate everything. Opposite of traditional on-prem patterns that favored vertical scaling and tight integration.

questions.scenario: A small team is used to planning server capacity 18 months in advance. In AWS they're told they can 'stop guessing capacity.' They want to understand how this works in practice.
questions.question: Which AWS capability BEST enables 'stop guessing capacity'?
choices: Reserved Instances, which lock in capacity for 1 or 3 years
AWS Auto Scaling, which adds or removes resources based on real-time demand
AWS Trusted Advisor, which warns about over-provisioning
AWS Budgets, which alerts on unexpected spend
questions.explanation:
AWS Auto Scaling (for EC2, ECS, DynamoDB, Aurora, etc.) scales capacity in and out automatically based on real demand signals. Instead of predicting demand, you react to it. That's the operational embodiment of 'stop guessing capacity.'

Reserved Instances (A) require you to GUESS capacity — 1 or 3 year commitment. Trusted Advisor (C) recommends best practices but doesn't scale anything. Budgets (D) is cost monitoring. The trap: RIs sound capacity-related, but they're a commitment tool, not an elastic scaling tool.
questions.pattern: Six cloud advantages: trade CapEx for OpEx, economies of scale, **stop guessing capacity** (via elasticity + Auto Scaling), speed/agility, stop running data centers, go global in minutes. Auto Scaling family: EC2 ASG + Application Auto Scaling (ECS, DynamoDB, Aurora RR) + predictive scaling.

questions.scenario: A CTO explains cloud service models to a non-technical board. Three tiers: one where the customer manages the application only, one where the customer manages the OS and above, and one where the customer manages nothing except configuration.
questions.question: Which mapping is correct?
choices: IaaS = manage only configuration; PaaS = manage OS and above; SaaS = manage application only
IaaS = manage OS and above; PaaS = manage application only; SaaS = manage only configuration
IaaS = manage application only; PaaS = manage only configuration; SaaS = manage OS and above
IaaS, PaaS, and SaaS all require managing the OS
questions.explanation:
IaaS (Infrastructure as a Service) — customer manages guest OS, middleware, runtime, data, and application. AWS example: EC2. PaaS (Platform as a Service) — customer manages only the application and data; provider handles OS, runtime, middleware. AWS example: Elastic Beanstalk, Lambda (more extreme PaaS). SaaS (Software as a Service) — customer uses the application; provider handles everything. AWS example: Amazon WorkMail, WorkDocs; external example: Salesforce.

Mnemonic: the further up the stack the service model, the less the customer manages. IaaS = most work, most control. SaaS = least work, least control. PaaS in the middle.
questions.pattern: Service model stack (customer responsibility shrinks as you go up): IaaS (EC2) → CaaS (Fargate) → PaaS (Beanstalk, App Runner, Lambda) → SaaS (Chime, WorkMail). Each step up = less OS/runtime management, less flexibility. Shared responsibility shifts accordingly.

questions.scenario: A company evaluates three deployment models: fully on AWS, fully on-premises, and a model where some workloads stay in their data center while others run in AWS with connectivity between the two.
questions.question: What is the third model called?
questions.explanation:
Hybrid cloud combines on-premises (or private cloud) with public cloud, connected via Direct Connect or Site-to-Site VPN. Enterprises use it to keep sensitive or latency-critical workloads on-prem while running new ones cloud-native.

Multi-cloud (A) means two or more public clouds (AWS + Azure) — not on-prem + one cloud. Private cloud (C) is all-on-premises or dedicated single-tenant infra. Community cloud (D) is shared infra among organizations with common requirements (e.g., government agencies) — rare in practice.
questions.pattern: Deployment models: public (all AWS), private (all on-prem), hybrid (on-prem + public, connected), multi-cloud (multiple public clouds). Hybrid connectivity: Direct Connect (dedicated fiber), Site-to-Site VPN (over internet, IPsec), AWS Outposts (AWS hardware on-prem), Local Zones/Wavelength (AWS edge near users).

questions.scenario: A startup provisions new staging environments in minutes to test features, versus months of procurement on-premises. This speed unlocks a new culture of experimentation.
questions.question: Which cloud benefit does this BEST represent?
questions.explanation:
Agility (also called 'speed and agility') is the benefit describing how quickly new resources and environments can be provisioned — minutes vs months. It enables cheap experimentation: spin up, test, tear down.

Elasticity (A) is automatic scale up/down with demand — about runtime resource adjustment, not provisioning new environments. Durability (B) is data not being lost over time (S3 = 11 nines). Fault tolerance (D) is continuing to operate despite failures. The trap: elasticity and agility both involve speed, but elasticity = runtime scaling; agility = provisioning speed and experimentation.
questions.pattern: Cloud-benefit vocabulary: Agility = provisioning speed + experimentation. Elasticity = auto scale with demand. Scalability = architectural growth capacity. High availability = uptime. Durability = data not lost. Fault tolerance = operates through failure. Memorize the keyword map — CLF tests it constantly.

questions.scenario: A finance team compares AWS to their previous data center. On-prem required large upfront hardware purchases. AWS lets them pay only for what they use each month, with no long-term commitments required.
questions.question: Which cloud benefit does this describe?
choices: Trade capital expense for variable expense
questions.explanation:
Moving from capital expense (upfront hardware purchases) to variable/operating expense (pay-per-use, no upfront) is one of the six foundational cloud benefits. The CFO story: don't guess and buy hardware — rent exactly what you use.

Go global in minutes (A) is about multi-region deployment speed. Economies of scale (C) is AWS passing lower per-unit costs to customers because of its massive scale. Speed and agility (D) is provisioning velocity. All are real cloud benefits, but 'pay only for what you use + no upfront' is specifically the CapEx-to-OpEx benefit.
questions.pattern: Six advantages of cloud (memorize — CLF favorite): (1) Trade CapEx for variable expense. (2) Benefit from economies of scale. (3) Stop guessing capacity. (4) Increase speed and agility. (5) Stop spending on running data centers. (6) Go global in minutes.

questions.scenario: A security engineer wants an application running on an EC2 instance to access S3 WITHOUT embedding long-lived AWS credentials on the instance.
questions.question: What is the correct mechanism?
choices: Store access keys in an environment variable on the EC2 instance
Attach an IAM role to the EC2 instance via an instance profile
Create an IAM user for the EC2 instance and authenticate as that user
Use the AWS root account credentials for the EC2 instance
questions.explanation:
IAM roles attached to EC2 (via instance profile) provide temporary, automatically-rotated credentials delivered through the instance metadata service (IMDS). The SDK and CLI retrieve them transparently. No keys on disk, no rotation to manage, no leakage from embedded secrets.

Option A is the classic anti-pattern — keys leak via logs, snapshots, compromised hosts. Option C (IAM user per instance) means long-lived credentials and operational misery. Option D is a catastrophic security violation — root should never be used routinely. The trap: IAM users feel like 'the identity for machines,' but for AWS workloads, **roles** are the mechanism.
questions.pattern: IAM identity types: users (humans with console/programmatic access), groups (collections of users), roles (assumable identity for services, federation, cross-account). Rule: any AWS service calling other AWS services uses a role. EC2 → role via instance profile. Lambda → execution role. ECS task → task role. Never embed long-lived access keys.

questions.scenario: A company prepares for a compliance audit. The auditor asks for evidence of AWS's SOC 1, SOC 2, ISO 27001, and PCI DSS certifications to support the customer's own compliance attestation.
questions.question: Which service provides on-demand access to these AWS compliance reports?
questions.explanation:
AWS Artifact is the no-cost, self-service portal for downloading AWS's compliance reports (SOC, ISO, PCI DSS, FedRAMP, IRAP, etc.) and agreements (BAA, GDPR DPA). It's where auditors and compliance teams get the current attestation documents.

Trusted Advisor (A) gives best-practice recommendations, not compliance documents. Audit Manager (C) helps the CUSTOMER collect evidence for THEIR audit against frameworks (PCI, HIPAA, SOC 2) — it automates evidence collection from AWS resources but doesn't provide AWS's own reports. Config (D) tracks resource configuration compliance against YOUR rules. The trap: Audit Manager sounds like 'audit reports,' but it builds YOUR audit package, not AWS's.
questions.pattern: Compliance toolkit: Artifact = AWS's own compliance reports (download SOC/ISO/PCI). Audit Manager = automate YOUR audit evidence against PCI/HIPAA/SOC 2 frameworks. Config = resource config compliance against your rules. Security Hub = consolidated findings. Trusted Advisor = best-practice checks. Two different things: AWS's compliance reports vs. your compliance posture.

questions.scenario: A developer's laptop is compromised. The developer had programmatic access to AWS via IAM user access keys. The security team needs to minimize blast radius immediately.
questions.question: Which action is MOST effective as an immediate response?
choices: Change the AWS root account password
Deactivate and delete the compromised IAM user's access keys, and audit CloudTrail for suspicious activity
Enable MFA on the root account
Terminate all EC2 instances in the account
questions.explanation:
The specific attack vector is the compromised IAM user's access keys. Deactivating them immediately cuts off the attacker's programmatic access; deleting them ensures they can't be reactivated; auditing CloudTrail reveals what was done while keys were live. Textbook key-compromise incident response.

Option A doesn't help if root wasn't compromised. Option C (MFA on root) is a preventative best practice, not a response to THIS compromise. Option D (terminate all EC2) is extreme, causes self-inflicted outage, and doesn't address credential-level persistence — the attacker may have touched S3, RDS, IAM, Lambda too. The trap: 'compromise' makes candidates think broadly, but scope the response to the compromised identity first, then audit.
questions.pattern: IAM compromise response: (1) revoke the specific credentials (deactivate + delete access keys, force password reset, disable MFA if suspect). (2) audit CloudTrail for actions taken with those credentials. (3) check for newly-created users/roles/policies (attacker persistence). (4) rotate any secrets exposed. (5) post-mortem. Always: MFA on root and high-privilege users, and never use root for day-to-day work.

references.title: What to do if you inadvertently expose an AWS access key

questions.scenario: A security team wants to continuously detect compromised EC2 instances, crypto-mining activity, and anomalous API calls across multiple AWS accounts. They don't want to deploy agents.
questions.question: Which service fits this requirement?
questions.explanation:
GuardDuty is AWS's intelligent threat-detection service. It continuously analyzes CloudTrail events, VPC Flow Logs, and DNS logs (all log sources AWS already has — **no agent required**) to detect compromised instances, crypto-mining, reconnaissance, and unusual API activity. Multi-account via AWS Organizations.

Inspector (A) scans EC2 instances and ECR images for software vulnerabilities (CVEs). It's a vuln scanner, not a threat detector, and Inspector v2 uses the SSM agent on EC2. Shield (C) is DDoS protection. Macie (D) discovers and classifies sensitive data in S3 (PII, PHI). The trap: Inspector and GuardDuty both 'detect bad things,' but Inspector = known vulnerabilities (static); GuardDuty = active threats from behavior (dynamic).
questions.pattern: Threat detection vs vulnerability detection: GuardDuty = threats from behavior (log analysis, agentless). Inspector = software CVEs (scanning, uses SSM agent). Macie = S3 data classification (PII/PHI). Detective = threat investigation (deep graph on CloudTrail/VPC Flow/GuardDuty findings). Pairing: GuardDuty for detection, Detective for investigation, Inspector for vuln mgmt.

questions.scenario: A company must ensure certain S3 objects cannot be deleted or overwritten for a fixed retention period (e.g., 7 years) to meet regulatory requirements like SEC 17a-4.
questions.question: Which S3 feature meets this requirement?
choices: S3 Object Lock in Compliance mode
questions.explanation:
S3 Object Lock with Compliance mode provides WORM (Write Once Read Many) semantics. In Compliance mode, even the root account cannot delete or modify locked objects during the retention period — the strict mode designed for SEC 17a-4, FINRA, CFTC regulatory compliance. Governance mode is the softer variant (privileged users with special permissions can override).

Versioning (A) preserves version history but doesn't prevent permanent deletion of versions. Bucket policies (C) can be modified by admins, so not a true immutability control. Lifecycle policies (D) transition or expire objects over time — opposite of retention. The trap: versioning + MFA delete gets close, but Object Lock Compliance is the explicit WORM compliance answer.
questions.pattern: S3 data protection layers: Versioning (multiple versions per key, soft delete via markers, MFA delete hardens deletion). Object Lock (Governance = privileged override; Compliance = strict WORM, even root can't delete during retention). Replication (CRR/SRR) for durability. Encryption for confidentiality. For regulatory WORM ⇒ Object Lock Compliance mode.

questions.scenario: A company wants to centrally manage encryption keys for multiple AWS services across multiple accounts, audit every use of every key, and enforce annual key rotation.
questions.question: Which service provides these capabilities?
questions.explanation:
KMS is the managed service for creating, managing, and using encryption keys. Every use is logged to CloudTrail (full auditability), automatic annual rotation can be enabled on customer-managed keys, and keys can be shared across accounts via key policies and grants. KMS integrates natively with nearly all AWS services (S3, EBS, RDS, Lambda, etc.).

ACM (A) manages TLS/SSL certificates for HTTPS — not general encryption keys. Secrets Manager (C) stores secrets (passwords, API keys), uses KMS under the hood, but is about secrets, not key management itself. CloudHSM (D) provides dedicated single-tenant HSMs for FIPS 140-2 Level 3 requirements — more expensive, specialty use. The trap: CloudHSM sounds like 'the most secure option' but is the right answer only when dedicated HSMs are specifically required.
questions.pattern: Key/secret management: KMS = managed encryption keys (AWS-managed, customer-managed, imported, multi-Region). CloudHSM = dedicated FIPS 140-2 L3 HSMs (specialty/expensive). Secrets Manager = secret values (passwords, DB creds) with rotation. Parameter Store = config + secrets (cheap, no rotation). ACM = TLS certs.

questions.scenario: A security engineer must decide between AWS IAM Identity Center (formerly AWS SSO) and creating IAM users for granting employees access to multiple AWS accounts.
questions.question: Which statement CORRECTLY describes the recommended approach for multi-account human access?
choices: Create IAM users in every account for each employee — provides the best audit trail
Use IAM Identity Center to federate users from an external IdP (or its built-in directory) and map them to permission sets across accounts
Share the root account credentials across the team
Create one IAM user per employee in the management account and give them access keys to assume roles
questions.explanation:
IAM Identity Center is AWS's recommended identity solution for multi-account human access. Users authenticate once (federated from your IdP — Okta, Azure AD, Google Workspace, or Identity Center's internal store), then get short-lived credentials to each account via permission sets. Centralized management, MFA enforcement, SSO.

Option A (IAM user per account per employee) is the painful old way — N accounts × M employees = credential sprawl. Option C is catastrophic — root must never be shared. Option D is an improvement but still relies on long-lived IAM user credentials in the management account; Identity Center delivers temporary credentials without persistent IAM users. The trap: IAM users feel like 'the' primary identity, but for humans in multi-account setups, Identity Center is current best practice.
questions.pattern: Human-access architecture: (best) IAM Identity Center + external IdP → permission sets → short-lived creds to any account in Organization. (legacy) IAM users + assume-role into member accounts. (worst) root / shared creds / per-account IAM users. SAML federation works directly too, but Identity Center reduces setup.

questions.scenario: An online retailer wants to migrate their self-managed Cassandra cluster to a fully managed AWS service that preserves CQL (Cassandra Query Language) compatibility so existing application code keeps working.
questions.question: Which service fits?
questions.explanation:
Amazon Keyspaces is AWS's managed, serverless Cassandra-compatible service. It speaks CQL, so existing Cassandra drivers and application code work with minimal changes. Fully managed, auto-scaling, pay-per-request or provisioned capacity.

DynamoDB (A) is AWS's proprietary NoSQL — excellent service but NOT Cassandra-compatible. Migration from Cassandra to DynamoDB requires application rewrites. DocumentDB (C) is MongoDB-compatible (document/JSON), not Cassandra (wide-column). Neptune (D) is a graph database. The trap: DynamoDB is AWS's marquee NoSQL, many default to it for any 'NoSQL workload' — but CQL compatibility requires Keyspaces.
questions.pattern: Purpose-built database map: DynamoDB (key-value/document, AWS-native API), Keyspaces (Cassandra/CQL), DocumentDB (MongoDB/JSON), Neptune (graph/Gremlin+SPARQL), ElastiCache Redis (in-memory), MemoryDB (Redis + durable), Timestream (time-series), QLDB (ledger/immutable), Aurora (relational MySQL/Postgres-compatible).

questions.scenario: A fintech company needs an immutable, cryptographically verifiable transaction ledger. They must be able to prove mathematically to auditors that records have not been altered since they were written.
questions.question: Which service is purpose-built for this?
choices: Amazon RDS for PostgreSQL with a trigger-based audit table
questions.explanation:
QLDB is a purpose-built ledger database: immutable, append-only history with a SHA-256 hash chain (the journal) that provides cryptographic proof no record has been tampered with. Designed specifically for system-of-record audit use cases — financial transactions, supply chain, claims, DMV records.

RDS with triggers (A) can log changes but is NOT cryptographically verifiable and is tamperable by DB admins. Managed Blockchain (C) is for multi-party decentralized scenarios (Hyperledger Fabric, Ethereum) where no single party owns the ledger — overkill when a single company owns the data. S3 Object Lock (D) gives WORM for files but isn't a ledger — you'd have to build ledger semantics on top. The trap: Blockchain feels like the 'immutable ledger' answer by reflex, but QLDB is correct for single-owner ledgers with centralized trust.
questions.pattern: Ledger use cases: single owner needing immutable + cryptographic proof ⇒ QLDB. Multi-party trustless ledger (consortia, public chains) ⇒ Managed Blockchain. WORM files for compliance ⇒ S3 Object Lock. Database audit logs with less rigor ⇒ CloudTrail + app-level logging. QLDB gives digests you can hand to auditors for mathematical proof.

questions.scenario: A media company encodes thousands of videos per month. Each job takes 30-90 minutes, is fault-tolerant (can restart), and arrives in unpredictable bursts. They want a managed service to queue, schedule, and run these jobs on cost-optimized compute.
questions.question: Which service is PURPOSE-BUILT for this pattern?
choices: Amazon ECS on EC2 with Auto Scaling
questions.explanation:
AWS Batch is the managed service for batch computing at any scale. It handles job queueing, scheduling, and dynamic provisioning of compute resources (EC2 or Fargate, on-demand or Spot — Batch can auto-target Spot for cost). Bursty arrival + long jobs + fault tolerance is its sweet spot.

Lambda (A) has a hard 15-minute max runtime — 30-90 min jobs are impossible. ECS on EC2 with Auto Scaling (C) could work but you'd build job queueing, scheduling, retry logic yourself — Batch does all of it. Step Functions (D) orchestrates workflows across services, it's not a batch compute engine. The trap: 'batch processing' as a concept vs AWS Batch the service — both match here, but the product-specific answer is Batch. For video specifically, MediaConvert is even more purpose-built.
questions.pattern: Compute selection by job shape: <15 min event-driven ⇒ Lambda. Long-running always-on services ⇒ ECS/EKS/Fargate. Bursty, fault-tolerant, long-running jobs ⇒ AWS Batch (with Spot for cost). Complex multi-step workflows ⇒ Step Functions (orchestrator). Media-specific: Elemental MediaConvert for video transcoding.

questions.scenario: A web application in a VPC calls Amazon S3 thousands of times per hour. The architects want traffic to stay on the AWS private network, never traverse the public internet, and NOT incur NAT Gateway data-processing charges.
questions.question: Which solution BEST meets these requirements?
choices: NAT Gateway in a public subnet
questions.explanation:
Gateway VPC Endpoints for S3 (and DynamoDB) provide private connectivity from a VPC to those services via route-table entries. No data-processing charge, no NAT Gateway, no hourly endpoint fee — essentially free. Traffic stays on the AWS network.

NAT Gateway (A) works but you pay the NAT hourly charge PLUS $0.045/GB data-processing fee, and traffic leaves the VPC for S3's public endpoint. Interface VPC Endpoint (C) uses PrivateLink and IS supported for S3 (added later), but charges hourly + per-GB — more expensive than Gateway Endpoint for same-VPC S3 access unless you specifically need DNS from on-prem or cross-VPC shared services. Direct Connect (D) is on-prem-to-AWS, not VPC-to-S3. The trap: Interface Endpoint sounds more 'modern,' but for S3 within one VPC, Gateway Endpoint is the right cheap answer.
questions.pattern: VPC private access to AWS services: Gateway Endpoints (S3 and DynamoDB ONLY, free, route-table based). Interface Endpoints / PrivateLink (most other AWS services; ENI in subnet, hourly + per-GB, supports DNS + on-prem access). For S3 inside one VPC ⇒ Gateway Endpoint. For S3 from many VPCs/on-prem with DNS resolution ⇒ Interface Endpoint.

questions.scenario: An Auto Scaling Group sits behind an Application Load Balancer. The team wants unhealthy instances to be detected and replaced automatically, and no traffic routed to them in the meantime.
questions.question: Which health-check combination is REQUIRED?
choices: Only EC2 status checks on each instance
Only ALB target group health checks
EC2 status checks AND ALB target group health checks, with Auto Scaling configured to use ELB health checks
No health checks needed — AWS automatically detects failed instances
questions.explanation:
Two layers cooperate: ALB target group health checks determine whether the load balancer sends traffic to an instance (application-level health). Auto Scaling group health checks determine whether the ASG replaces an instance. By default, ASG uses only EC2 status checks, but you should add 'ELB' to the ASG health check types so an ALB-unhealthy instance triggers replacement too. Without this, an app crash that doesn't crash the instance will stop ALB routing but nothing replaces the broken instance.

Option A misses the app layer — EC2 status checks only detect hardware/OS reachability. Option B stops traffic but nothing replaces. Option D is wrong — AWS doesn't magically know your app is broken. The trap: candidates assume either layer alone is sufficient; both are needed, and ASG must trust the ELB verdict.
questions.pattern: Health-check layers: EC2 status checks (hardware + reachability, AWS-provided). ALB/NLB target health checks (your app endpoint, determines routing). ASG health check type (EC2 default; add 'ELB' for replacement on LB-unhealthy). Route 53 health checks (DNS-level). Proper pattern: ALB target health + ASG with 'ELB' enabled.

references.title: ELB health checks for Auto Scaling

questions.scenario: A small team wants to deploy a simple WordPress site with a managed database, SSD storage, and predictable monthly cost. They don't want to configure VPCs, security groups, or load balancers.
questions.question: Which service is MOST appropriate?
choices: Amazon EC2 with manual WordPress install
questions.explanation:
Amazon Lightsail is AWS's simplified VPS offering: preconfigured stacks (WordPress, LAMP, Node.js, etc.) bundled with storage, data transfer, and a static IP at a flat monthly price starting at $3.50. No VPC/security-group configuration needed for basic use. Perfect for simple sites.

EC2 with manual install (A) works but requires VPC + security groups + EBS + AMI selection + stack setup — much more work, variable cost. Elastic Beanstalk (C) is PaaS for deploying applications — simplifies some infra but still more complex than Lightsail and has variable billing. Option D is fake. The trap: Beanstalk and Lightsail both simplify EC2, but Lightsail is the 'WordPress-in-a-box for $5/month' answer.
questions.pattern: Managed simplification services: Lightsail = VPS/WordPress-in-a-box, fixed monthly pricing. Elastic Beanstalk = PaaS deploying code to managed EC2/ASG/ELB. App Runner = fully managed container/web app service. Amplify = full-stack web/mobile (front-end + back-end + CI/CD). For simplest WordPress at fixed cost ⇒ Lightsail.

questions.scenario: A development team wants to deploy applications via Git push, with AWS handling build, test, and deploy phases, integrated with CodeCommit, and no servers to manage.
questions.question: Which pipeline of services provides the COMPLETE CI/CD experience?
choices: CodeCommit → CodeBuild → CodeDeploy, orchestrated by CodePipeline
CodeCommit → S3 → Lambda, orchestrated by Step Functions
GitHub → Jenkins → CloudFormation, deployed on EC2
questions.explanation:
The canonical AWS CI/CD stack: CodeCommit (managed Git) → CodeBuild (managed build/test) → CodeDeploy (managed deployment to EC2/Lambda/ECS/on-prem), with CodePipeline as the orchestrator modeling source → build → test → deploy phases.

Option B isn't CI/CD — it's event-driven compute. Option C mixes in non-AWS tools (GitHub, Jenkins) which work fine but don't match 'AWS to handle it all.' Option D mentions CodeStar (a project-management wrapper that uses the Code* services underneath — incomplete answer, and being de-emphasized in some regions). The trap: CodeStar sounds like the umbrella but the real backbone is Pipeline + Commit + Build + Deploy.
questions.pattern: AWS developer-tools suite: CodeCommit (Git), CodeBuild (build/test), CodeDeploy (deploy to EC2/Lambda/ECS), CodePipeline (orchestrate end-to-end), CodeArtifact (package repo), CodeGuru (code review + profiler), CodeStar (wrapper/project init). Full CI/CD: CodePipeline orchestrates CodeCommit → CodeBuild → CodeDeploy.

questions.scenario: A company wants to send push notifications, SMS messages, and emails to millions of users. They want a single service that can handle all three channels and segment users based on attributes.
questions.question: Which service fits?
questions.explanation:
Amazon Pinpoint is a customer engagement service supporting push, SMS, email, voice, and in-app messaging from one service, with user segmentation, campaign analytics, and A/B testing. Built for marketing/engagement use cases.

SNS (A) is a pub/sub messaging service supporting push + SMS + email — but it's transactional/fan-out, not a campaign tool with segmentation and analytics. SES (C) is email-only (highly scalable, cheap). Connect (D) is a cloud contact center / call center product — not for push/SMS/email campaigns. The trap: SNS and Pinpoint overlap on channels, but Pinpoint is the marketing/engagement tool, SNS is the simple pub/sub messaging primitive.
questions.pattern: Messaging/engagement services: SNS = pub/sub (fan-out, push/SMS/email, transactional). Pinpoint = marketing/engagement (push/SMS/email/in-app, segmentation, campaigns, analytics). SES = transactional email at scale. Chime SDK = video/voice/messaging for apps. Connect = cloud contact center. 'Campaign with segmentation' ⇒ Pinpoint. 'Simple fan-out' ⇒ SNS.

questions.scenario: A company's AWS bill jumped 40% unexpectedly. Finance wants to identify which team or project drove the increase. Their accounts don't map one-to-one with teams — engineers share resources across teams.
questions.question: Which AWS mechanism is FOUNDATIONAL for enabling per-team chargeback/showback analysis?
choices: Enable consolidated billing and rely on per-account totals
Apply a consistent cost allocation tag strategy, activate the tags in the Billing console, and analyze in Cost Explorer
Open a support case with AWS to manually split the bill
Use separate AWS accounts per team — it's the only way to track spend
questions.explanation:
Cost allocation tags are the AWS-native mechanism for splitting shared-account spend by team, project, environment, or any dimension. You tag resources consistently (e.g., `Team=backend`, `Project=checkout`, `Env=prod`), activate the tags as **cost allocation tags** in the Billing console (crucial step — raw tags aren't automatically billing-visible), and then Cost Explorer and the Cost and Usage Report filter and group by those tags.

Option A (account-level totals) can't tell you which team spent what within a shared account. Option C isn't how AWS works — AWS won't manually split bills. Option D (one account per team) is a valid architectural approach but extreme and not 'foundational' — the question is about a team that has one account with shared resources. The trap: separate accounts is a legit answer for strict isolation, but for the stated scenario, cost allocation tags are the foundation.
questions.pattern: Cost-allocation foundations: (1) Consistent tagging strategy organization-wide (enforced via tag policies in Organizations). (2) Activate tags as cost allocation tags in the Billing console. (3) Analyze via Cost Explorer (tag filter/group). (4) Deep-dive via Cost and Usage Report in Athena/QuickSight. Alternatively: account-per-team for hard isolation (Control Tower helps scale this). Tags + multiple accounts complement each other.

questions.scenario: A company commits to $100/hour of compute spend for 3 years via Compute Savings Plans. They want to understand where the savings applies.
questions.question: Which statement is CORRECT about Compute Savings Plans coverage?
choices: Applies only to the specific EC2 instance type chosen at commitment
Applies to EC2, Fargate, and Lambda usage, regardless of instance family, size, region, or OS
Applies only to EC2 in a single region
Applies to EC2 and RDS database instances
questions.explanation:
Compute Savings Plans offer the most flexibility: they apply to EC2 (any instance family, size, region, OS, tenancy), Fargate, AND Lambda compute, up to 66% savings vs On-Demand. You commit to $X/hour for 1 or 3 years.

Option A describes a Reserved Instance (Standard RI is instance-specific). Option C describes a regional RI with no cross-region flexibility. Option D mixes in RDS — RIs exist for RDS but Savings Plans don't cover RDS (RDS has its own Reserved Instances). The trap: there are TWO types of Savings Plans — Compute (the flexible one covering EC2/Fargate/Lambda) and EC2 Instance (cheaper savings but locked to a specific family in a specific region). Easy to confuse.
questions.pattern: Purchasing options ladder: On-Demand (no commit). Spot (interruptible, up to 90% off). Reserved Instances (1 or 3 yr, instance-specific; EC2/RDS/ElastiCache/Redshift/OpenSearch). Savings Plans — Compute (EC2/Fargate/Lambda, any family/region, up to 66% off). Savings Plans — EC2 Instance (specific family in a region, up to 72% off). Dedicated Hosts (licensing/compliance). SageMaker Savings Plans (ML-specific).

questions.scenario: A company wants to centralize billing across 30 AWS accounts AND apply preventive guardrails that block specific regions and specific services org-wide. They also want consolidated CloudTrail logs across accounts.
questions.question: Which combination meets ALL three requirements?
choices: AWS Organizations with consolidated billing only — it handles all three
AWS Organizations with all features enabled, using SCPs for region/service guardrails and an organization trail in CloudTrail
A single AWS account with multiple IAM roles — simpler and achieves the same outcome
AWS Control Tower only — it replaces the need for Organizations
questions.explanation:
AWS Organizations with **all features enabled** unlocks SCPs (Service Control Policies) that preventively block specific regions, services, or actions org-wide — no matter what IAM permissions a member account's admins grant, SCPs win. Organization trails in CloudTrail collect events from all accounts into a central bucket. Consolidated billing is always included with Organizations. Three requirements, one Organization (with all features) satisfies all three.

Option A (consolidated billing only mode) lacks SCPs — all-features mode is required for SCPs. Option C (one account) abandons the multi-account isolation model. Option D is misleading — Control Tower RUNS ON TOP of Organizations (it sets up a landing zone using Organizations + config/guardrails) and does not replace Organizations; it extends it with opinionated defaults. The trap: Control Tower and Organizations are sometimes confused — Organizations is the primitive, Control Tower is the opinionated setup layer.
questions.pattern: Multi-account governance stack: AWS Organizations (accounts + OUs + consolidated billing + SCPs if all features enabled). Control Tower (landing zone on top — uses Organizations + Config + CloudTrail + SSO/Identity Center with opinionated guardrails). SCPs = preventive guardrails, applied at root/OU/account level, override IAM allow. Organization Trail = central CloudTrail for all accounts. Config aggregator = central Config view.

questions.scenario: An auditor asks where AWS operates. The company needs to explain the difference between Regions, Availability Zones, Edge Locations, and Local Zones.
questions.question: Which statement CORRECTLY describes AWS's global infrastructure?
choices: Edge Locations are large geographic areas; Regions are small caches for CloudFront
Regions contain multiple isolated Availability Zones; Edge Locations are CloudFront PoPs for content caching; Local Zones extend AWS infrastructure to metro areas closer to end users
Availability Zones are single data centers in one building; Regions are clusters of AZs in the same building
Local Zones are the same as Wavelength Zones — both are for 5G workloads only
questions.explanation:
Regions are geographic areas (e.g., us-east-1). Each Region contains multiple isolated AZs (each AZ is one or more physically separated data centers with independent power/cooling/networking, connected by low-latency private fiber). Edge Locations are CloudFront points of presence (hundreds globally) for caching. Local Zones extend AWS infrastructure (compute, storage, DB) to metro areas closer to end users for ultra-low latency. Wavelength Zones are specifically embedded in 5G carrier networks.

Option A reverses the relationship. Option C is wrong — AZs are physically separated, often miles apart, not in one building. Option D is wrong — Local Zones are for metro-proximity latency, while Wavelength is specifically 5G carrier-embedded. The trap: Local Zones and Wavelength both 'extend AWS closer to users' but serve different purposes.
questions.pattern: Global infra hierarchy (memorize): Region (geographic area, e.g., eu-west-1) → AZ (isolated DC cluster within region, 3+ per region) → local racks. Plus: Edge Locations (600+ CloudFront PoPs, global DNS via Route 53, WAF/Shield), Local Zones (metro-proximity compute/storage), Wavelength Zones (inside 5G carrier networks), Outposts (AWS hardware physically ON customer's premises).

questions.scenario: A company wants to understand the ONE thing that differentiates 'cloud computing' from traditional hosting.
questions.question: Which attribute is MOST characteristic of cloud computing per NIST and AWS definitions?
choices: On-demand self-service provisioning over a network with rapid elasticity and measured service
Running virtual machines instead of physical servers
Renting hardware from a data center instead of buying it
Using open-source software for all workloads
questions.explanation:
NIST's canonical cloud-computing definition (and AWS's marketing framing) identifies five essential characteristics: (1) on-demand self-service, (2) broad network access, (3) resource pooling, (4) rapid elasticity, (5) measured service (pay-per-use). Option A captures the core distinguishing attributes.

Option B (VMs) is a common technology of clouds but not the definition — bare-metal cloud offerings exist, and many non-cloud systems use VMs. Option C (renting hardware) describes colocation, not cloud — colo doesn't provide on-demand self-service. Option D (open source) is unrelated — AWS runs many proprietary technologies. The trap: candidates pick what cloud commonly IS (option B/C) instead of what DEFINES it.
questions.pattern: NIST cloud essentials (CLF quotes these regularly): On-demand self-service = provision without human interaction. Broad network access = available via standard networks. Resource pooling = multi-tenant shared infrastructure. Rapid elasticity = scale up/down quickly. Measured service = metered usage for billing. These five plus the three service models (IaaS/PaaS/SaaS) and four deployment models (public/private/hybrid/community) are the whole framework.
