questions.scenario: A CTO is estimating total cost of ownership (TCO) of running on AWS vs their current data center. The CFO asks which cost categories AWS TCO analysis typically eliminates or reduces.
questions.question: Which categories are MOSTLY ELIMINATED or significantly reduced when moving to AWS?
choices: Server hardware purchases, data center facility costs (power, cooling, space), and hardware refresh cycles
Software licensing costs for all third-party applications
questions.explanation:
AWS TCO analysis focuses on the costs that migrating ELIMINATES (physical hardware purchases, data center facilities, power/cooling/space leases, hardware refresh every 3-5 years, physical security). Staff time shifts from hardware maintenance toward higher-value work; licensing may change (some BYOL, some AWS-managed); development cost remains.

Option B (licensing) is partly true (RDS/EMR/managed services include licensing, and some BYOL options) but overstated — you still pay for third-party software like Oracle Database, Windows Server (though AWS offers included licensing), SAP, etc. Option C (all staff) is wrong — you still need developers, architects, SRE. Option D (app dev costs) is unrelated to the infra move. The TCO calculator compares infra cost categories specifically.
questions.pattern: AWS TCO categories: SERVER (hardware purchase + warranty + depreciation), STORAGE (SAN/NAS/backup), NETWORK (switches, load balancers, firewalls), DATA CENTER (real estate, power, cooling, physical security), IT LABOR (hardware ops). AWS move typically eliminates most of these, trades for variable pay-per-use. Use AWS Pricing Calculator for estimates.

questions.scenario: A team wants to 'go global in minutes' — deploy their app to users in Europe, Asia, and North America quickly. They use Route 53 latency-based routing to direct users to the nearest region.
questions.question: Which cloud advantage BEST matches this scenario?
questions.explanation:
'Go global in minutes' is one of the six advantages of cloud — deploying an application in multiple Regions worldwide with low latency and redundancy, provisioned within minutes rather than the months it takes to establish overseas data centers.

Economies of scale (A) is about lower per-unit costs from AWS's purchasing power. Stop guessing capacity (C) is about elastic scaling to actual demand. Variable expense (D) is pay-per-use vs CapEx. All are real cloud advantages — but the SPECIFIC 'deploy worldwide fast' answer is 'go global in minutes.' The trap: candidates overlap the six advantages; memorize the specific keyword for each.
questions.pattern: Six cloud advantages keywords: (1) CapEx → OpEx = 'variable expense'. (2) Economies of scale = 'massive scale of AWS shared with you'. (3) Stop guessing capacity = 'elastic, match demand'. (4) Speed & agility = 'provision in minutes, experiment'. (5) Stop spending on running data centers = 'focus on differentiating work'. (6) Go global in minutes = 'multi-region deployment fast'.

questions.scenario: A team wants to use AWS resources for experimentation — trying new architectures, tearing them down, trying again. They want to make sure their infrastructure setup is REPRODUCIBLE from code.
questions.question: Which AWS approach BEST enables this reproducibility?
choices: Click through the AWS Console and document each step in a wiki
Use Infrastructure as Code via AWS CloudFormation or AWS CDK
Email AWS Support to recreate environments on request
questions.explanation:
Infrastructure as Code (IaC) — AWS CloudFormation (declarative YAML/JSON templates) or AWS CDK (imperative code in TypeScript/Python/Java/C#/Go that compiles to CloudFormation) — lets you define infrastructure in code, version it in Git, and deploy identical copies on demand. Tear down and recreate reliably.

Option A (Console + wiki) is the anti-pattern — drift, human error, slow, not reproducible. Option C isn't how AWS Support works. Option D (snapshots) is for data recovery, not infrastructure topology reproducibility. The trap: candidates know snapshots exist and think they qualify as 'infrastructure' — but a snapshot captures data/disk state, not the wiring of VPCs, ASGs, IAM, etc.
questions.pattern: IaC on AWS: CloudFormation (YAML/JSON, declarative, AWS-native, stacks/change sets), CDK (code-defined, transpiles to CFN), Terraform (HashiCorp, multi-cloud, external tool), SAM (Serverless Application Model, CFN wrapper for Lambda/API Gateway). IaC enables: reproducibility, drift detection, versioning in Git, peer review, rollback, multi-region copies.

questions.scenario: A company is evaluating which AWS services are globally scoped vs regionally scoped. Their architecture team wants to avoid surprises when deploying to a new region.
questions.question: Which of the following is a GLOBAL service (not region-scoped)?
questions.explanation:
AWS IAM is a global service — users, groups, roles, and policies are defined once per AWS account and are visible/usable in all regions. CloudFront, Route 53, IAM, and AWS Organizations are the classic 'global' services.

EC2 (A) is region-scoped — instances exist in a specific region and AZ. S3 buckets (B) have a global NAMESPACE but buckets are CREATED in a specific region (data stored there) — this is the most common trick question: 'S3 is global' is false; 'S3 bucket names are globally unique' is true, but buckets are regional. RDS (D) is region-scoped. The trap: S3's global bucket namespace confuses candidates into calling S3 'global' — but data residency is regional.
questions.pattern: AWS service scope: GLOBAL = IAM, Route 53, CloudFront, WAF (when attached to CloudFront/Global Accelerator), AWS Organizations. REGIONAL (vast majority) = EC2, S3 (buckets created in a region), RDS, Lambda, DynamoDB (tables in a region, but Global Tables span regions), VPC. AZ-scoped = subnets, EBS volumes. Critical nuance: S3 bucket names are globally unique, but each bucket IS regional.

questions.scenario: A startup uses AWS for prototypes and often spins up resources that they forget to clean up. Costs are creeping up. They want a cultural/operational practice to address this.
questions.question: Which Well-Architected pillar MOST addresses 'don't forget to turn things off' discipline?
questions.explanation:
Cost Optimization's core practices include matching supply to demand, right-sizing, expenditure awareness, and using managed services. 'Adopt a consumption model' — pay only for what you use — explicitly pairs with 'stop paying for things you don't use.' Automated shutdown of idle resources (dev/test outside work hours, deleting unused EBS volumes and snapshots, stopping forgotten instances) is a Cost Optimization pattern.

Reliability (A) is recovery/availability. Sustainability (C) also promotes reducing unused resources, but from an environmental angle; the CLF framing here is cost-driven. Operational Excellence (D) is broad (run/monitor/improve), overlaps but isn't the primary owner of the 'forgot to turn it off' problem. The trap: Sustainability and Cost Optimization both target waste — for a cost-focused scenario, pick Cost Optimization.
questions.pattern: Cost Optimization design principles: adopt consumption model (pay-per-use), measure overall efficiency (understand cost per business outcome), stop spending on undifferentiated heavy lifting (use managed services), analyze/attribute expenditure (tagging), use managed services to reduce TCO. Tools: Cost Explorer, Budgets, Trusted Advisor, Compute Optimizer, CUR for deep analysis.

questions.scenario: A company is evaluating the AWS Cloud Adoption Framework (CAF). Leadership asks which 'perspective' within the CAF addresses skills, organizational structure, and change management.
questions.question: Which CAF perspective MOST directly addresses people, skills, and change?
questions.explanation:
The AWS CAF organizes cloud transformation into six perspectives: Business, People, Governance, Platform, Security, Operations. The **People** perspective specifically addresses roles/skills, organizational structure, leadership, workforce transformation, and training — the 'human side' of cloud adoption.

Platform (A) covers architecture, engineering, and platform experience. Operations (B) covers running cloud workloads (observability, performance, incident response). Governance (D) covers portfolio management, program/project management, benefits realization. The trap: candidates see 'change management' and may pick Governance (which does include change), but the SKILLS + ROLES + ORG STRUCTURE focus is the People perspective.
questions.pattern: CAF six perspectives: BUSINESS (outcomes, value realization), PEOPLE (roles, skills, org, training, change), GOVERNANCE (portfolio, program, benefits, risk), PLATFORM (architecture, data, CI/CD), SECURITY (identity, detection, response, compliance), OPERATIONS (observability, performance, incident, continuity). Mnemonic: B-P-G-P-S-O.

questions.scenario: A company is mapping AWS services to the traditional stack. Someone asks: what's the AWS equivalent of a 'physical network switch' for routing between subnets in a VPC?
questions.question: Which VPC component is the logical analog of a router between subnets?
questions.explanation:
The route table is the core VPC primitive that decides how traffic flows between subnets, to the internet gateway, to NAT gateways, to VPC endpoints, to peering connections, and to transit gateways. Each subnet is associated with exactly one route table. Route tables are the logical routers in a VPC.

Internet Gateway (A) is the egress/ingress point to the public internet — attached to the VPC, referenced in a route table for public subnets. ENI (C) is a network interface attached to an instance (virtual NIC) — about instance connectivity, not inter-subnet routing. NAT Gateway (D) is specific — it translates private → public for outbound internet, not general routing. The trap: candidates pick IGW because it 'routes to internet,' but the primitive that DECIDES where packets go is the route table.
questions.pattern: VPC primitives: VPC (isolated network), Subnets (AZ-bound slices), Route Tables (logical routers per subnet), Internet Gateway (public egress/ingress), NAT Gateway (private → public egress only), VPC Endpoints (private connectivity to AWS services), Security Groups (stateful, instance-level firewall), NACLs (stateless, subnet-level firewall), ENIs (virtual NICs).

questions.scenario: A healthcare company is deciding whether AWS is 'HIPAA compliant' so they can store Protected Health Information (PHI) in S3 and run workloads on EC2.
questions.question: Which statement is CORRECT about AWS and HIPAA?
choices: AWS is 'HIPAA certified' and any service can store PHI without restriction

AWS provides HIPAA-eligible services that customers can use to run HIPAA-compliant workloads, but the customer is responsible for configuring them compliantly and must sign an AWS Business Associate Addendum (BAA)
HIPAA compliance is entirely AWS's responsibility — the customer only needs to use AWS
AWS cannot be used for HIPAA workloads
questions.explanation:
AWS offers HIPAA-eligible services (S3, EC2, RDS, Lambda, and many more) and will sign a Business Associate Addendum (BAA) with customers that governs PHI handling. However, HIPAA compliance under the shared responsibility model is a partnership — AWS secures the infrastructure; the customer configures services compliantly (encrypt PHI in transit/at rest, restrict access with IAM, audit via CloudTrail, segregate environments). Not every AWS service is HIPAA-eligible; only those listed as such can handle PHI.

Option A (HIPAA certified, any service) is wrong — there's no such thing as 'HIPAA certified'; HIPAA is a compliance program, not a certification. Option C dumps all responsibility on AWS (classic shared-responsibility fail). Option D is wrong — AWS absolutely supports HIPAA workloads. The trap: 'HIPAA-compliant cloud' is a marketing shorthand; the actual answer is eligible services + BAA + customer configuration.
questions.pattern: Compliance on AWS: AWS supplies compliance PROGRAMS (SOC 1/2/3, ISO 27001, PCI DSS, HIPAA, FedRAMP, etc.) via AWS Artifact. Customer must still: choose eligible services, sign BAAs where required (HIPAA), configure controls, audit via CloudTrail + Config + Security Hub, document in their own compliance program. Shared responsibility applies.

questions.scenario: A company wants prescriptive guidance from AWS for deploying a baseline multi-account environment with governance, identity, logging, and guardrails built in from day one.
questions.question: Which service provides this opinionated multi-account landing zone?
questions.explanation:
AWS Control Tower provides an opinionated 'landing zone' — a multi-account AWS environment with best-practice guardrails (preventive via SCPs, detective via Config rules), centralized logging (CloudTrail + Config to a log archive account), identity (integrated with IAM Identity Center), and an Account Factory for provisioning new accounts. It sits ON TOP OF Organizations + Config + CloudTrail + Identity Center with defaults.

Organizations (A) is the primitive that Control Tower uses — it manages accounts, OUs, SCPs, but doesn't set up the opinionated landing zone automatically. Systems Manager (C) is for operational management (Patch Manager, Session Manager, Run Command). Config (D) tracks resource configuration. The trap: Organizations alone is necessary but not sufficient for a 'baseline landing zone with governance' — Control Tower adds the opinions and defaults.
questions.pattern: Multi-account governance stack: AWS Organizations (accounts, OUs, SCPs, consolidated billing — primitive). AWS Control Tower (landing zone on top — log archive account, audit account, guardrails, Account Factory, IAM Identity Center integration). Builders hierarchy: Organizations first; Control Tower if you want the opinionated setup; Customizations for Control Tower (CfCT) to extend.

questions.scenario: A team migrates an application from on-prem to AWS. They lift-and-shift the app as-is onto EC2, then later refactor it to use managed services (RDS, Lambda, SQS). These represent two of the AWS 'migration strategies.'
questions.question: Which two strategies does this describe?
questions.explanation:
The AWS '7 Rs' migration strategies: **Rehost** ('lift and shift' — move to EC2 as-is), **Replatform** ('lift, tinker, and shift' — minor optimization like moving DB to RDS), **Refactor/Re-architect** (redesign to use cloud-native patterns like serverless, microservices, managed services), Repurchase (move to SaaS), Retire (decommission), Retain (keep on-prem), Relocate (move VMs to VMware Cloud on AWS without conversion).

Option A (Rehost → Replatform) is close but Replatform is minor tweaks, not the full rewrite to managed services described. The scenario describes moving to serverless/managed — that's Refactor. Options C and D are wrong combinations. The trap: Replatform and Refactor both involve changes, but Replatform is 'lift, tinker, and shift' (small) while Refactor is 'redesign' (large).
questions.pattern: AWS 7 Rs: REHOST (lift-and-shift to EC2, fastest, least benefit). RELOCATE (VMware Cloud on AWS, VM-level move). REPLATFORM (lift-tinker-shift: DB → RDS, OS upgrades). REPURCHASE (switch to SaaS, e.g., CRM → Salesforce). REFACTOR/RE-ARCHITECT (redesign using cloud-native). RETIRE (shut down). RETAIN (keep on-prem). Effort and cloud-native benefit both increase left-to-right.

questions.scenario: A developer is confused about what an IAM policy actually is and how it differs from a policy attached to a resource.
questions.question: Which statement is CORRECT about IAM policy types?
choices: Identity-based policies are attached to users/groups/roles and define what THAT identity can do; resource-based policies are attached to resources (like S3 buckets) and define who can access THAT resource
Identity-based policies and resource-based policies are interchangeable — you can attach either type to any entity
Resource-based policies only exist for EC2 instances
All IAM policies are identity-based; there are no resource-based policies
questions.explanation:
Identity-based policies attach to IAM principals (users, groups, roles) and specify what actions that identity can perform on what resources. Resource-based policies attach directly to resources (S3 bucket policies, Lambda resource policies, SNS topic policies, KMS key policies, etc.) and specify who can perform which actions on THAT resource. Both policy types are evaluated together at request time.

Option B is wrong — they're structurally similar but attach to different things. Option C is wrong — resource-based policies exist for many services: S3, KMS, SNS, SQS, Lambda, ECR, API Gateway, Secrets Manager, etc. Option D is wrong. The trap: candidates don't realize resource-based policies exist, and think 'all permissions come from IAM users.'
questions.pattern: IAM policy types: Identity-based (attach to user/group/role — 'what can this identity do'). Resource-based (attach to resource — 'who can access this resource'). Permissions boundaries (cap the max permissions an identity can have). SCPs (Organization-level preventive guardrails). Session policies (pass in AssumeRole). ACLs (legacy, mostly S3/VPC). All combine in evaluation: explicit DENY always wins; SCP must allow; identity AND resource policy are OR'd (grant from either).

questions.scenario: A company wants a firewall that filters HTTP/HTTPS requests to their web application based on IP reputation, geographic origin, SQL injection patterns, and XSS patterns.
questions.question: Which service is DESIGNED for this?
questions.explanation:
AWS WAF (Web Application Firewall) is a layer-7 firewall that filters HTTP/HTTPS requests based on rules: IP-based, geographic, rate-based, managed rule groups for SQL injection (SQLi) and cross-site scripting (XSS), and custom regex. Attaches to CloudFront, ALB, API Gateway, App Runner, AppSync.

Security Groups (A) and NACLs (B) operate at layers 3/4 (IP + port + protocol) — they don't inspect HTTP payloads. Shield Standard (D) is DDoS protection (layer 3/4 attack mitigation, free on all AWS accounts) — it doesn't inspect application-layer content. The trap: candidates conflate 'protects web app' → 'any AWS security service,' but the layer-7 HTTP-aware answer is WAF specifically.
questions.pattern: Layered security: SGs/NACLs (L3/L4 IP+port, VPC-level). WAF (L7 HTTP, inspects request content, rule-based). Shield Standard (L3/L4 DDoS, free, always on). Shield Advanced (enhanced DDoS + SRT support + cost protection, $3000/month). GuardDuty (threat detection from logs). Firewall Manager (centrally manage WAF/Shield/SGs across accounts).

questions.scenario: A company's VPC has a private subnet containing an EC2 instance. The security team wants to SSH into the instance WITHOUT exposing any SSH ports to the internet and WITHOUT running a bastion host.
questions.question: Which AWS service provides this?
questions.explanation:
Systems Manager Session Manager provides browser-based or CLI shell access to EC2 instances (and on-prem servers) with no inbound ports open, no bastion, no SSH keys to manage. Instances need the SSM agent (preinstalled on Amazon Linux 2, Amazon Linux 2023, Windows AMIs) and an IAM role allowing Session Manager. All sessions are logged to CloudTrail (and optionally to S3/CloudWatch Logs).

Direct Connect (B) is a dedicated network link on-prem → AWS, not a shell-access tool. WorkSpaces (C) is virtual desktops for end users, not EC2 access. Client VPN (D) gives remote users network access to a VPC via OpenVPN — you'd still need SSH afterward, still need an SSH port open. The trap: Client VPN solves network connectivity, but Session Manager is the 'zero open ports' answer.
questions.pattern: Remote access patterns: (traditional) bastion host in public subnet + SSH key. (better) EC2 Instance Connect for temporary SSH. (best) Systems Manager Session Manager — no inbound ports, IAM-controlled, CloudTrail-audited, works across SSH/RDP/PowerShell. For database port forwarding, use Session Manager port forwarding or AWS Client VPN.

questions.scenario: An enterprise wants to automatically discover and protect sensitive data like credit card numbers, PII, and PHI stored in thousands of S3 buckets across their organization.
questions.question: Which service is PURPOSE-BUILT for this?
questions.explanation:
Amazon Macie uses machine learning and pattern matching to discover, classify, and protect sensitive data in S3 — PII (names, emails, addresses), financial data (credit card numbers, bank accounts), credentials, and regulated data types (PHI, intellectual property). It inventories S3 buckets, flags public/unencrypted ones, and generates findings.

GuardDuty (B) is threat detection (compromised instances, unusual API calls). Config (C) tracks resource configuration changes. Inspector (D) scans EC2/ECR for CVEs. The trap: all four are 'security' services, easy to confuse — but Macie is specifically for sensitive-data discovery in S3.
questions.pattern: Security-service purpose matrix: GuardDuty = threat detection from logs (CloudTrail/VPC Flow/DNS). Inspector = vuln scanning on EC2/ECR/Lambda. Macie = sensitive-data classification in S3. Detective = investigate findings (graph analytics). Security Hub = aggregate findings. Config = resource config + compliance rules. IAM Access Analyzer = unintended external access paths.

questions.scenario: A developer accidentally commits AWS access keys into a public GitHub repository. AWS's automated scanning detects the leak.
questions.question: What happens AUTOMATICALLY (before human intervention)?
choices: Nothing — AWS waits for the customer to respond
AWS attaches a quarantine policy (AWSCompromisedKeyQuarantine) to the IAM user restricting the keys, notifies the account root email, and opens a support case
AWS deletes the IAM user to contain the breach
questions.explanation:
When AWS detects exposed credentials (via its credential-scanning partnership with GitHub and other platforms), it automatically attaches a quarantine policy — `AWSCompromisedKeyQuarantineV2` — to the compromised IAM user. This policy blocks high-risk actions (like creating new users, launching expensive EC2 instances) while allowing the customer to rotate the keys. AWS simultaneously emails the account root and opens a support case alerting the customer.

Option A is wrong — AWS takes automatic protective action. Option C is wrong — AWS doesn't delete the user (that could cause outages). Option D is invented. The trap: candidates don't know AWS has this scanning program and auto-quarantine response.
questions.pattern: AWS credential-leak response: (automatic) quarantine policy attached, root notification email, support case opened. (customer required) rotate the keys, audit CloudTrail for misuse, delete unused resources the attacker created, rotate anything the attacker might have touched. Long-term fix: eliminate long-lived access keys — use IAM roles for services, IAM Identity Center for humans, temporary credentials via STS.

references.title: What to do if you inadvertently expose an AWS access key

questions.scenario: A company wants to encrypt data AT REST in an S3 bucket. They want AWS to manage the encryption keys entirely, with no customer key management overhead. They also want the encryption to be automatic on every PUT.
questions.question: Which encryption option fits?
choices: SSE-S3 (Server-Side Encryption with Amazon S3-managed keys)
Client-side encryption with a customer-managed KMS key
SSE-KMS with a customer-managed key and manual rotation
questions.explanation:
SSE-S3 uses S3-managed encryption keys (AES-256) with AWS handling everything — automatic encryption on PUT, decryption on GET, no customer key management at all. Since January 2023, all new S3 objects are encrypted with SSE-S3 by default (unless another SSE type is specified). Zero overhead, no KMS charges.

SSE-C (B) requires the customer to provide encryption keys on every request — the customer manages keys, not 'no overhead.' Client-side encryption (C) requires customer code to encrypt before uploading — maximum overhead. SSE-KMS with customer-managed key (D) offers audit trail and IAM-based key policies but has KMS API call overhead/costs and requires key management. The trap: many candidates pick SSE-KMS by default because it's the fanciest option, but for 'AWS manages everything with no overhead,' SSE-S3 is right.
questions.pattern: S3 encryption options: SSE-S3 (default, AES-256, AWS-managed keys, free, zero overhead). SSE-KMS (KMS-managed keys, customer-managed or AWS-managed, CloudTrail audit of key use, KMS API charges). SSE-C (customer provides keys on every request — used when customer wants full key custody but lets S3 do encryption). CSE (client-side encryption with KMS or external key — max control, max effort). DSSE-KMS (dual-layer encryption for strict compliance).

questions.scenario: A developer writes an IAM policy that explicitly allows `s3:*` on a bucket. An SCP at the Organization level denies `s3:DeleteObject` on that bucket. The developer tries to delete an object.
questions.question: What happens?
choices: The delete succeeds because the IAM policy explicitly allows it
The delete is denied because an SCP explicit DENY overrides any IAM allow
The delete succeeds because SCPs only apply to the management account
The delete is denied because IAM default-deny blocks the action
questions.explanation:
In AWS's policy evaluation logic, explicit DENY always wins — whether it comes from an IAM policy, a resource policy, a permissions boundary, or an SCP. SCPs are preventive guardrails that act as a filter on the maximum permissions any member-account identity can have; an IAM allow cannot exceed what the SCP permits. Net: delete is denied.

Option A misses SCP precedence. Option C is wrong — SCPs apply to all accounts under the Organization (except the management account, where they're not enforced on root by default, which is its own gotcha). Option D is wrong — IAM default-deny is overridden by an explicit allow, so if no SCP existed, the delete would succeed. The trap: candidates think IAM is the final authority, but SCPs + permissions boundaries bound it.
questions.pattern: IAM evaluation order (simplified): (1) Explicit DENY anywhere → denied. (2) SCP must explicitly allow (implicit deny in SCP = denied). (3) For cross-account: resource-based policy OR identity-based policy must allow. (4) For same-account: identity-based policy must allow (or resource policy in same account). Permissions boundaries cap identity policies. Session policies cap sessions. DENY always wins.

questions.scenario: A company must provide end users access to an internal web app without exposing it on the public internet. Users are on corporate laptops and mobile devices. They want identity-aware access, no VPN client, and per-request authorization.
questions.question: Which service fits?
questions.explanation:
AWS Verified Access is a zero-trust application-access service — users authenticate via an identity provider (IAM Identity Center, Okta, JumpCloud, etc.) and, on every request, Verified Access evaluates identity and device posture against a policy (Cedar-based) before granting access to the app. No VPN client required, per-request authorization, works for private apps.

Client VPN (A) gives users full network access to a VPC via OpenVPN — traditional VPN, not zero-trust, not identity-aware per-request. Site-to-Site VPN (C) connects networks (e.g., corporate DC ↔ VPC), not users. WorkSpaces (D) is virtual desktops. The trap: Client VPN feels like the right answer for 'remote users,' but Verified Access is the newer, zero-trust, per-request-authz answer.
questions.pattern: Remote/app access: Client VPN (network-level VPN for users, OpenVPN-based). Site-to-Site VPN (network-to-network IPsec). Direct Connect (dedicated fiber, on-prem to AWS). AWS Verified Access (zero-trust app access, identity + device posture per request, no VPN client). WorkSpaces (virtual desktops). AppStream 2.0 (stream desktop apps to browser). Transit Gateway (hub for multi-VPC/VPN connectivity).

questions.scenario: A company wants to detect and respond to changes in AWS resource configurations that violate their standards — e.g., an S3 bucket becoming public, a security group opening port 22 to 0.0.0.0/0, or an unencrypted EBS volume being created.
questions.question: Which service is DESIGNED for this?
choices: AWS Config with managed or custom rules
questions.explanation:
AWS Config records configuration state of AWS resources over time AND evaluates resources against rules (managed rules like `s3-bucket-public-read-prohibited`, `encrypted-volumes`, `restricted-ssh`, or custom Lambda-backed rules). Non-compliant resources can trigger SNS notifications or automated remediation via Systems Manager Automation documents.

CloudTrail (A) is an API audit log — records who called what, but doesn't evaluate configuration against rules. CloudWatch metrics (C) is for performance/operational metrics, not configuration compliance. Trusted Advisor (D) has a security section with fixed checks but isn't customizable and isn't focused on continuous compliance evaluation. The trap: CloudTrail + EventBridge rules CAN detect config changes, but Config is purpose-built for 'resource-configuration compliance' with pre-built and custom rules.
questions.pattern: Audit/compliance services: CloudTrail = API audit (who did what, when, from where). Config = resource CONFIGURATION state + compliance rules. CloudWatch = metrics + logs + alarms (operational). Config Conformance Packs = bundled compliance rulesets (e.g., PCI DSS, HIPAA). Security Hub = aggregate findings across Config/GuardDuty/Inspector/Macie. Use Config for 'is my S3 bucket public?'; use CloudTrail for 'who made it public?'

questions.scenario: An application generates thousands of time-series metrics from IoT sensors — temperature, pressure, location over time. They need a purpose-built database for high-ingest, fast time-range queries, and automatic rollup of older data.
questions.question: Which service fits BEST?
questions.explanation:
Amazon Timestream is AWS's managed, serverless time-series database. Designed for high ingest (millions of events/second), fast time-range queries, and automatic tiered storage — recent data in memory (hot), older in magnetic (cold, cheaper) — with configurable retention per tier. Built-in analytics functions (moving averages, interpolation).

DynamoDB (A) can store time-series data but isn't optimized for it — you'd need careful partition key design to avoid hot partitions, and you'd lose built-in time-range functions. RDS PostgreSQL (C) works but won't scale to millions-of-events-per-second ingest without painful sharding. DocumentDB (D) is document/JSON for MongoDB compatibility, not time-series. The trap: DynamoDB is everyone's go-to NoSQL answer, but time-series has a purpose-built option.
questions.pattern: Purpose-built database decision: time-series ⇒ Timestream. Graph ⇒ Neptune. Ledger/immutable ⇒ QLDB. Document (MongoDB) ⇒ DocumentDB. Wide-column (Cassandra) ⇒ Keyspaces. Key-value ⇒ DynamoDB. In-memory ⇒ ElastiCache / MemoryDB. Relational ⇒ Aurora/RDS. Data warehouse ⇒ Redshift. Each is optimized for a specific access pattern — using the wrong one requires workarounds.

questions.scenario: A company runs an analytics workload that scans petabytes of data in S3 to answer ad-hoc SQL queries. They want a serverless service that charges per query, with no cluster to manage.
questions.question: Which service is MOST appropriate?
questions.explanation:
Athena is serverless, pay-per-query (per TB scanned) SQL over data in S3. No cluster to manage, no warehouse to size, no servers. Perfect for ad-hoc analytics and log querying. Uses Presto/Trino under the hood, integrates with Glue Data Catalog for schema.

Redshift Serverless (A) is also serverless but is a data-WAREHOUSE — you ingest data into Redshift storage (or use Redshift Spectrum to query S3). Great for repeated complex queries and BI dashboards at scale, but more overhead than Athena for truly ad-hoc one-offs. EMR (C) is managed Hadoop/Spark/Presto clusters — not serverless (though EMR Serverless now exists). Aurora Serverless (D) is a relational OLTP database, not for petabyte S3 analytics. The trap: Athena vs Redshift — Athena for ad-hoc S3 SQL; Redshift for ongoing analytical workloads with loaded data.
questions.pattern: Analytics service map: Athena = serverless SQL on S3 (ad-hoc, pay-per-query). Redshift = data warehouse (ongoing, high-performance analytics, columnar). Redshift Spectrum = query S3 from Redshift. EMR = managed big-data frameworks (Spark/Hadoop/Presto/Hive, clustered). Glue = serverless ETL + Data Catalog. Kinesis = real-time streaming. OpenSearch = search + log analytics. QuickSight = BI dashboards.

questions.scenario: A company wants to stream real-time clickstream data from their website (millions of events per second) and process it with low latency for real-time dashboards.
questions.question: Which service is PURPOSE-BUILT for real-time data streaming at scale?
questions.explanation:
Kinesis Data Streams is AWS's real-time data-streaming service — designed for high-throughput ingest (MB/s per shard, scalable to thousands of shards), sub-second latency, data retention 1-365 days for replay, multiple concurrent consumers (Lambda, Kinesis Data Analytics, Kinesis Data Firehose, custom consumers). Textbook use case.

SQS (B) is a queue for decoupling — it's not a streaming service with multi-consumer replay semantics. Multiple consumers on the same SQS queue compete for messages; with Kinesis, each consumer sees all records. S3 event notifications (C) trigger downstream services per object PUT — not a streaming-ingest service. Step Functions (D) orchestrates workflows, not streaming. The trap: SQS and Kinesis both 'handle messages,' but queue vs stream differ — queue = single-consumer-per-message; stream = multi-consumer + replay.
questions.pattern: Messaging/streaming families: SQS (queue, point-to-point, pull, at-least-once or exactly-once for FIFO). SNS (pub/sub, push to multiple subscribers, at-least-once). EventBridge (event bus, rules + filtering across SaaS/AWS). Kinesis Data Streams (real-time streaming, multi-consumer + replay, sharded). Kinesis Data Firehose (managed delivery stream: buffer + load into S3/Redshift/OpenSearch). Kinesis Data Analytics / Managed Service for Apache Flink (stream processing SQL/Flink). MSK (managed Kafka).

questions.scenario: A legacy application uses a Network File System (NFS) mount to share files between multiple Linux servers. The company wants to move to AWS while preserving the shared NFS semantics.
questions.question: Which AWS service is the DIRECT equivalent?
choices: Amazon FSx for Windows File Server
questions.explanation:
Amazon EFS is a fully managed, scalable NFS file system for Linux workloads. Multiple EC2 instances (or ECS/EKS tasks, Lambda functions, on-prem servers via Direct Connect/VPN) can mount the same EFS file system concurrently. Native NFS v4.x protocol. Perfect for lift-and-shift of NFS-based apps.

S3 (A) is object storage, not a POSIX file system — applications expecting NFS semantics (file locking, directory traversal performance, POSIX permissions) won't work without rewriting. EBS (B) is block storage for a single EC2 instance (EBS Multi-Attach exists for io1/io2 but with clustering-filesystem caveats). FSx for Windows (D) speaks SMB, not NFS. The trap: candidates see 'shared file system' and pick S3 or EBS without matching the protocol.
questions.pattern: Storage-protocol map: Block (EBS, instance-attached). File — Linux/NFS (EFS). File — Windows/SMB + AD (FSx for Windows). File — HPC/Lustre (FSx for Lustre). File — multi-protocol hybrid (FSx for NetApp ONTAP, FSx for OpenZFS). Object (S3). Hybrid gateway (Storage Gateway). Match protocol before picking.

questions.scenario: A company runs EC2 instances and wants to reduce cost by using Spot Instances for 70% of the fleet while keeping 30% as On-Demand to guarantee baseline capacity. They want this handled automatically by a single Auto Scaling Group.
questions.question: Which feature enables this mixed purchasing strategy in ONE ASG?
choices: EC2 Auto Scaling with mixed instances policy (combining On-Demand and Spot)
AWS Batch with managed compute environment
EC2 Fleet with target capacity of 100
Two separate ASGs behind the same ALB
questions.explanation:
EC2 Auto Scaling supports a 'mixed instances policy' that lets a single ASG contain both On-Demand and Spot Instances, with configurable percentages (e.g., 30% On-Demand base, 70% Spot for cost). You also specify multiple instance types for Spot diversification. Scaling events consider the mix.

Option B (AWS Batch) is for batch jobs, not application ASGs. Option C (EC2 Fleet) is related — Fleet is a programmatic launch API that can launch mixed On-Demand/Spot capacity, but it's not the Auto Scaling Group pattern for long-running app tiers (ASG + mixed instances is the right answer for 'application behind a load balancer'). Option D (two ASGs) works but is MORE complex than a mixed-instances ASG. The trap: EC2 Fleet and ASG mixed-instances overlap conceptually; for 'single ASG' the answer is mixed-instances policy.
questions.pattern: EC2 purchasing strategies: Pure On-Demand = simple, most expensive. Pure Spot = cheap but interruptible. Mixed ASG = base On-Demand + burst Spot, best of both for stateless tiers. Savings Plans or RIs for the On-Demand portion. For batch jobs: AWS Batch with Spot. For HPC/ML: EC2 Fleet + Spot placement strategies.

references.title: Auto Scaling groups with multiple instance types

questions.scenario: An application serves static content and dynamic API responses. The team wants low latency for global users, HTTPS termination at the edge, and protection from DDoS — via AWS's CDN.
questions.question: Which service provides all of these?
questions.explanation:
Amazon CloudFront is AWS's content delivery network (CDN). 600+ edge locations globally. It caches static content, accelerates dynamic origin requests, terminates HTTPS at the edge with ACM certificates, and benefits from AWS Shield Standard DDoS protection (included free). Integrates with WAF for app-layer security.

Route 53 (A) is DNS + health checks, not content delivery. Global Accelerator (C) provides static Anycast IPs and AWS-backbone routing for non-HTTP traffic or when you want 2 static IPs for fast failover — it's not a CDN (no caching). API Gateway (D) manages APIs but isn't a global CDN. The trap: Global Accelerator and CloudFront both 'improve global latency' but differently — CloudFront caches HTTP content at edges; Global Accelerator provides fast, stable entry points to regional backends for any protocol.
questions.pattern: Global edge services: CloudFront = CDN (HTTP/HTTPS caching, dynamic origin acceleration, WAF integration, Shield). Route 53 = DNS (latency/geo/weighted/failover routing, health checks). Global Accelerator = static Anycast IPs + AWS backbone routing (great for TCP/UDP apps, games, VoIP, fast failover across regions). Outposts/Local Zones/Wavelength = AWS compute close to specific physical locations. Shield Standard = DDoS protection (included).

questions.scenario: A company runs a Windows application that uses Active Directory for authentication. They want to move to AWS while keeping AD-based authentication for Windows servers, SQL Server, and Windows file shares.
questions.question: Which service provides managed Active Directory on AWS?
choices: AWS Directory Service (AWS Managed Microsoft AD)
questions.explanation:
AWS Directory Service offers AWS Managed Microsoft AD — a fully managed Microsoft Active Directory running in AWS. Actual Microsoft AD (not a compatible alternative), so Windows servers, SQL Server, FSx for Windows, WorkSpaces, and other AD-dependent workloads work natively. AWS handles DC patching, multi-AZ, backups.

IAM (A) is AWS's own identity system for AWS APIs — not Active Directory. IAM Identity Center (C) is AWS's SSO portal for human access to AWS accounts and some SaaS apps — NOT a replacement for Active Directory for Windows workloads. Cognito (D) is for consumer app authentication (user pools for mobile/web apps, OAuth/OIDC). The trap: IAM Identity Center and AD sound like they should be the same thing — they aren't. Managed AD is for your Windows infrastructure; Identity Center is for SSO access to AWS.
questions.pattern: Identity services on AWS: IAM = AWS API access (users, roles, policies). IAM Identity Center = human SSO to AWS accounts + SaaS apps. Directory Service (AWS Managed Microsoft AD) = real Active Directory for Windows workloads. AD Connector = proxy to on-prem AD (no DCs in AWS). Simple AD = basic, Samba-based (compatibility subset, cheap). Cognito User Pools = consumer app identity. Cognito Identity Pools = federated guest/user identity → AWS credentials.

questions.scenario: A company needs to notify their engineers of production issues across phone, email, and SMS — fanning out one alert to many subscribers.
questions.question: Which service is the SIMPLEST fit?
questions.explanation:
SNS (Simple Notification Service) is pub/sub — publish once, fan out to many subscribers over various protocols: email, SMS, HTTP/HTTPS endpoints, mobile push (APNS/FCM), SQS queues, Lambda, Kinesis Firehose. Textbook alerting use case. CloudWatch Alarms and many AWS services integrate directly with SNS.

SES (A) is email-only (transactional email service). Pinpoint (C) is for customer marketing campaigns — overkill for internal alerting. Chatbot (D) integrates CloudWatch/SNS with Slack/Chime/MS Teams channels — useful alongside SNS but not a standalone fan-out messaging service. The trap: Pinpoint supports more channels but is a campaign product; for simple alert fan-out, SNS is the right simple answer.
questions.pattern: Notification service selection: SNS = simple pub/sub fan-out (email/SMS/push/HTTP/SQS/Lambda). SES = transactional email at scale. Pinpoint = marketing/engagement with segmentation. EventBridge = event routing with rules/filtering (more complex logic). Chatbot = SNS→Slack/Chime/Teams. For 'ops alert fan-out' ⇒ SNS.

questions.scenario: A company wants to run the same workload in multiple AWS regions for disaster recovery. The RDS database in their primary region must be replicated to a secondary region with minimal lag, and they need to be able to promote the secondary to primary if the primary region fails.
questions.question: Which RDS feature provides this?
choices: RDS snapshots copied to another region on a schedule
questions.explanation:
Cross-region read replicas for RDS (MySQL, MariaDB, PostgreSQL, Oracle, and all Aurora) asynchronously replicate from a primary to a secondary region. In a DR event, you can promote the cross-region replica to be the new primary (now a standalone database). Aurora Global Database is the even-lower-latency variant for Aurora specifically (<1 second cross-region lag, fast promotion).

Multi-AZ (A) is within a SINGLE region (HA across AZs, not cross-region DR). Snapshots on a schedule (C) have high RPO (you lose any data between snapshots) and slow restore time (RTO) — valid backup, not the right DR primitive for 'minimal lag.' RDS Proxy (D) is a connection pooler for reducing failover time and pool exhaustion, not a cross-region replication mechanism. The trap: Multi-AZ and cross-region replicas both provide 'standby DB' but serve different failure modes — Multi-AZ for AZ-level failure, cross-region for region-level DR.
questions.pattern: RDS/Aurora HA + DR ladder: Multi-AZ (synchronous standby, same region, automatic failover — HA not DR). Read replicas same-region (scale reads, can promote in a pinch). Cross-region read replicas (async, DR + global reads). Aurora Global Database (Aurora-specific, <1 second lag, primary + up to 5 secondary regions). Automated backups/snapshots (RPO: 5 min for Point-in-Time Recovery; daily snapshots). AWS Backup for centralized backup management.

references.title: Working with Amazon RDS read replicas

questions.scenario: A company's on-prem environment has 40 TB of data they want to seed into AWS and then keep synchronized incrementally going forward. The network link is 1 Gbps and they want an AWS-managed service to handle file transfer with scheduling, verification, and incremental sync.
questions.question: Which service fits BEST?
questions.explanation:
AWS DataSync is a managed data-transfer service for online transfers between on-prem (NFS/SMB/HDFS/object stores) and AWS (S3/EFS/FSx). It handles scheduling, incremental sync, integrity verification, bandwidth throttling, and parallelism — at up to 10 Gbps per agent. Perfect for ongoing sync scenarios. At 1 Gbps, 40 TB seeds in roughly 4 days (theoretical), manageable.

Snowball Edge (A) is a one-time physical shipment — great for the initial seed but no ongoing sync. Transfer Acceleration (C) speeds uploads to S3 via CloudFront edges — useful for interactive uploads but not a scheduled sync service. Storage Gateway Volume Gateway (D) presents iSCSI block storage backed by S3 — it's a storage gateway, not a sync tool. The trap: Snowball is tempting for 40 TB, but 'ongoing sync' rules it out; DataSync handles both initial + ongoing.
questions.pattern: Data-transfer service choice: Snow Family (Snowball Edge, Snowcone, Snowmobile-retired) = physical shipment for >10s of TB. DataSync = managed online sync (one-time + ongoing, NFS/SMB/HDFS → S3/EFS/FSx). Transfer Family (SFTP/FTPS/FTP managed endpoints to S3/EFS). Storage Gateway (hybrid gateway: File/Volume/Tape). S3 Transfer Acceleration = faster uploads via CloudFront. Direct Connect = dedicated network link. Database Migration Service (DMS) for databases specifically.

questions.scenario: A development team wants to build a serverless REST API that processes requests, calls a Lambda function, and stores data in DynamoDB. They want API Gateway to handle authentication, throttling, and API versioning.
questions.question: Which API Gateway REST API feature provides THROTTLING to prevent abuse?
choices: Usage plans with API keys and throttling limits
AWS WAF only — API Gateway has no native throttling
questions.explanation:
API Gateway REST APIs support usage plans that define throttle limits (steady-state request rate + burst) and quotas (requests per day/week/month) associated with API keys. Clients include their API key in the request header; API Gateway enforces limits and returns 429 Too Many Requests when exceeded. There's also account-level and method-level throttling defaults.

Option B is wrong — API Gateway has native throttling. Option C (Lambda concurrency) limits concurrent Lambda executions, which indirectly protects Lambda but doesn't give per-client API throttling. Option D (Route 53) has no rate-based routing feature. The trap: WAF can also rate-limit by IP, but it's additive — API Gateway's native usage plans are the primary per-client throttling mechanism.
questions.pattern: API Gateway features: authentication (IAM, Cognito, Lambda authorizer, JWT, API keys), throttling (usage plans + API keys, account/method defaults), caching (per-stage, GET responses), request/response transformation (mapping templates), stages (dev/test/prod) + canary deployments, CloudWatch metrics + access logging, WAF integration. REST API vs HTTP API vs WebSocket API — HTTP APIs are simpler/cheaper for basic proxy; REST has more features (usage plans, caching).

questions.scenario: A company has three AWS accounts: a management (payer) account and two member accounts A and B. Account A has $1000 of EC2 Reserved Instance coverage. An identical EC2 instance runs in account B with no RI. The consolidated billing feature determines whether the RI benefit applies.
questions.question: What happens to the RI discount?
choices: The RI discount stays in account A only; account B pays full On-Demand price
By default, RI discounts are shared across accounts in the Organization (if RI sharing is enabled), so account B can receive the benefit if a matching instance runs there
RI discounts never apply cross-account under any circumstances
RI discounts only apply to the management/payer account
questions.explanation:
Consolidated billing enables RI (and Savings Plan) sharing across the Organization by default. If account A has unused RI coverage (instance family, size, region, tenancy, OS match) and account B runs a matching instance, the RI discount is applied to account B's usage — 'unused' capacity in one account benefits another. RI sharing can be disabled per account if you want discounts strictly per account (e.g., for chargeback fairness).

Option A describes the case only if sharing is disabled. Option C is flatly wrong — sharing is ON by default. Option D is wrong — the discount applies to the matching usage regardless of which account runs the instance. The trap: candidates don't know RI sharing is on by default; finance teams sometimes disable it to maintain clean per-team cost attribution.
questions.pattern: Consolidated billing benefits: (1) Single invoice across all accounts. (2) Volume discounts aggregate (S3, CloudFront, EC2 per-hour tiered pricing). (3) RI and Savings Plan discounts shared across accounts by default (can be toggled per account in Billing). (4) Free-tier limits also shared in some cases. RI sharing is common 'gotcha' in multi-account chargeback — disable if teams buy their own RIs and want their own discounts.

questions.scenario: A startup CFO wants to set a hard upper limit on AWS spending. They want the account to STOP when spend reaches a threshold — so a runaway Lambda loop or forgotten GPU instance can't create a $100,000 surprise bill.
questions.question: Which statement CORRECTLY describes AWS's spending controls?
choices: AWS Budgets automatically stops services when the budget threshold is exceeded
AWS Budgets sends alerts when thresholds are exceeded; there is no AWS-native automatic hard stop, but Budget Actions can trigger IAM policies, EC2/RDS stops, or SSM automations to reduce usage
AWS Organizations includes a hard spending cap feature
Cost Explorer automatically terminates resources that exceed spend forecasts
questions.explanation:
AWS does NOT have a simple 'stop at $X' kill switch — resources keep running regardless of spend. AWS Budgets sends alerts (via SNS) when thresholds are hit, and AWS Budget Actions can automate responses: attach a restrictive IAM policy to a user/group, stop EC2 or RDS instances, run an SSM automation document. Still not a hard instantaneous stop, but it's the closest native mechanism. Customers often implement stricter controls via Lambda + EventBridge.

Option A is a common misconception — Budgets alerts, doesn't stop. Option C is wrong — Organizations governs accounts/SCPs/consolidated billing, not spending caps. Option D is wrong — Cost Explorer is analytics, nothing auto-terminates. The trap: candidates wish AWS had a kill switch and assume Budgets provides it.
questions.pattern: Cost-control toolkit: Cost Explorer (visual spend analytics, forecasts, anomaly detection). Budgets (alerts on cost/usage/RI/Savings Plan thresholds). Budget Actions (automate response: IAM restriction, stop EC2/RDS, SSM). Cost Anomaly Detection (ML-based surprise alerts). CUR (raw data feed for Athena/QuickSight). Service quotas to limit resource provisioning. SCPs to block expensive services/regions at Organization level. Tagging + allocation for chargeback.

references.title: Managing your costs with AWS Budgets

questions.scenario: An engineering manager wants to get an automated recommendation of which EC2 instance types they should right-size TO, based on their actual CPU/memory/network usage patterns.
questions.question: Which service provides these recommendations?
questions.explanation:
AWS Compute Optimizer uses machine learning on CloudWatch metrics to recommend right-sized EC2, Auto Scaling Groups, EBS volumes, Lambda functions, and ECS services on Fargate. It delivers specific SKU recommendations (e.g., 'your m5.2xlarge is underutilized — downsize to m5.large for $X savings') with confidence ratings.

Trusted Advisor (A) has a few fixed 'idle instance' and 'low utilization' checks — useful but less detailed than Compute Optimizer. CloudWatch (C) provides raw metrics but doesn't compute right-sizing recommendations. Cost Explorer (D) surfaces Savings Plan and RI recommendations but not instance right-sizing. The trap: Cost Explorer's RI recommendations and Trusted Advisor's low-utilization checks are related but Compute Optimizer is the purpose-built ML-recommendation service.
questions.pattern: Optimization recommenders: Compute Optimizer = ML-based right-sizing (EC2, ASG, EBS, Lambda, ECS Fargate). Trusted Advisor = broad best-practice checks (cost, security, fault tolerance, performance, service limits). Cost Explorer = RI + Savings Plan purchase recommendations. S3 Storage Lens = S3 usage analytics with recommendations. CloudWatch Logs Insights for log queries. Combine them: Compute Optimizer for right-sizing, Cost Explorer for commitment purchases, Trusted Advisor for broad health.

questions.scenario: A new AWS customer receives 12 months of 'AWS Free Tier' benefits. The CFO asks which of their expected usages are covered.
questions.question: Which pair CORRECTLY describes AWS Free Tier 12-month allocations?
choices: 5 GB S3 Standard storage + 750 hours/month of t2.micro EC2 (or t3.micro in Regions without t2.micro)
Unlimited EC2 + 1 TB S3 for 12 months
1 TB EBS storage + 1 million API Gateway requests per day
questions.explanation:
The AWS 12-month Free Tier for new accounts includes (among other things): 5 GB S3 Standard storage + 20,000 GET + 2,000 PUT/POST/LIST requests per month, and 750 hours/month of t2.micro or t3.micro EC2 (Linux or Windows). Plus RDS 750 hours/month of db.t2.micro, CloudFront 1 TB data transfer out, and similar small allocations. Option A captures the classic two.

Options B, C, and D are vastly exaggerated. The trap: candidates who have never read the actual Free Tier terms may pick the 'generous' option. The real Free Tier is modest — intended for learning/experimentation, not production.
questions.pattern: AWS Free Tier flavors: (1) 12-month (new accounts, first year only — EC2 t2/t3.micro 750h, RDS 750h, S3 5 GB, EBS 30 GB). (2) Always Free (ongoing, capped — Lambda 1M requests + 400k GB-seconds/month, DynamoDB 25 GB storage + 25 RCU/WCU, CloudWatch 10 metrics + 5 GB logs, etc.). (3) Short-term trials (e.g., Inspector 15-day, Redshift 2 months). Free Tier is for learning, not production capacity.

questions.scenario: A global engineering org wants to EDIT their AWS invoices to add internal project codes, re-group line items by business unit, and produce an internal bill that charges each business unit based on AWS usage plus an overhead markup.
questions.question: Which service is DESIGNED for this internal chargeback / bill customization?
questions.explanation:
AWS Billing Conductor is purpose-built for internal chargeback — it lets finance teams create custom 'billing groups' with their own custom pricing rules (markups, discounts, flat fees) to produce 'proforma' invoices for internal business units, separate from the actual AWS invoice. Exactly the 'add overhead markup and rebill internally' use case.

Cost Explorer (A) is analytics/reporting, not invoice modification. CUR (C) is a raw data feed of usage — foundation for analysis but not a rebilling engine. Budgets (D) is threshold alerting. The trap: this is a lesser-known service; most candidates default to CUR + custom scripts.
questions.pattern: Financial-ops toolkit: Billing Conductor = internal rebilling with markups/discounts for chargeback. Cost Explorer = spend analytics. CUR = raw data feed. Budgets = threshold alerts. Cost Categories = virtual groupings of costs by business meaning. Cost Allocation Tags = per-resource attribution. Savings Plan / RI recommendations inside Cost Explorer.

questions.scenario: A solutions architect must choose between a single large EC2 instance and multiple smaller EC2 instances for a stateless web tier. The goal is to handle variable load, survive instance failures, and keep costs tied to demand.
questions.question: Which architectural pattern is PREFERRED in the cloud?
choices: Vertical scaling on one large instance
Horizontal scaling across multiple smaller instances behind a load balancer, with Auto Scaling
Single dedicated host for tenancy control
Cluster placement group for low latency
questions.explanation:
Horizontal scaling (adding more smaller instances behind a load balancer, managed by an Auto Scaling Group) is the textbook cloud pattern for stateless tiers. Benefits: elasticity (scale with demand), fault tolerance (failure of one instance only affects part of capacity), and cost matching. Failure blast radius is small.

Vertical scaling (A) means ONE big instance — single point of failure, scaling requires downtime or replacement, no demand matching. Dedicated host (C) is for license/compliance reasons (tenancy control), not architectural scaling. Cluster placement group (D) is for HPC/low-latency needs, orthogonal. The trap: candidates pick 'one bigger instance' from on-prem habits; cloud best practice is many small things over few big things.
questions.pattern: Scaling patterns: Horizontal = add more instances (scale out/in) — cloud-preferred for stateless tiers. Vertical = bigger instance (scale up/down) — on-prem legacy, use for stateful workloads that can't be distributed. Combine: ASG with mixed instance types, behind ALB/NLB. Store state in managed services (RDS, DynamoDB, ElastiCache, S3) not on the instances.

questions.scenario: A CTO is asked during a board meeting: 'If AWS has an outage in us-east-1, does that mean our business dies?'
questions.question: Which architecture decision MOST mitigates single-Region outage risk?
choices: Deploying across multiple Availability Zones within us-east-1
Deploying to multiple Regions with Route 53 failover routing and cross-Region data replication
Enabling S3 Versioning on all buckets
questions.explanation:
Multi-Region deployment with Route 53 failover (active-passive or active-active) and cross-Region data replication (S3 Cross-Region Replication, DynamoDB Global Tables, Aurora Global Database, etc.) is the architectural defense against a Region-wide outage. Operationally expensive and complex, but the only answer that survives 'us-east-1 is down.'

Multi-AZ (A) survives AZ-level failure (common) but not region-wide failure (rare but happens — us-east-1 has had several notable incidents). Reserved Instances (C) are a pricing mechanism, not availability. S3 Versioning (D) protects against object deletion/overwrites, not region outage. The trap: many candidates think multi-AZ = DR, but multi-AZ = HA for single-region; multi-Region = DR for region-wide events.
questions.pattern: Availability hierarchy: single instance (not HA). Multi-AZ single region (HA baseline). Multi-Region active-passive (DR, higher RTO). Multi-Region active-active (DR + global low latency, most complex). Choose based on RTO/RPO and cost. DR strategies (from cheapest, slowest to costliest, fastest): Backup & restore, Pilot light, Warm standby, Multi-site active-active. For CLF: 'region outage' ⇒ multi-Region.

questions.scenario: A learning team is distinguishing the AWS documentation resources. They want to know which resource provides PRESCRIPTIVE, opinionated step-by-step guidance for common patterns — not a reference manual.
questions.question: Which AWS resource fits this description?
questions.explanation:
AWS Prescriptive Guidance is a dedicated portal of opinionated, step-by-step guides and patterns for common tasks (migration patterns, landing-zone setup, cloud architecture patterns, migration strategy guides). It's curated by AWS solutions architects and partners. Distinct from the reference documentation.

Service Documentation (A) is reference-oriented — API reference, feature docs, developer guides. It's comprehensive but not prescriptive. re:Post (C) is AWS's community Q&A site (replaced AWS Forums). Support Center (D) is for case management with AWS Support. The trap: all four are legitimate AWS resources; the 'prescriptive, opinionated, step-by-step' phrasing points specifically at Prescriptive Guidance.
questions.pattern: AWS documentation ecosystem: Service Documentation (docs.aws.amazon.com — reference, feature docs, API). Prescriptive Guidance (step-by-step patterns, opinions). Whitepapers (deep-dives on architecture, security, migration). re:Invent session videos (conference talks). AWS Skill Builder (training courses). AWS Workshops (hands-on guided labs). re:Post (community Q&A). AWS Solutions Library (ready-to-deploy reference implementations).

questions.scenario: A company has employees and contractors. Employees need console access to AWS to do their jobs. Contractors need short-term access to a specific AWS account for a 3-month project. Security wants centralized control and easy deprovisioning.
questions.question: Which approach is BEST?
choices: Create IAM users for all employees and contractors in each AWS account directly

Use AWS IAM Identity Center for employees (via federation from corporate IdP), and for contractors create time-bound permission sets or use IAM Identity Center with the contractor's account expiration managed centrally
Give contractors the root account credentials with a promise to delete them later
Share an IAM user's access keys across all contractors
questions.explanation:
IAM Identity Center supports federated access for both employees (via corporate IdP like Okta/Azure AD) and contractors (directly in Identity Center's internal directory OR federated from a contractor-specific IdP). Permission sets are time-bound where needed, and deprovisioning is a single action in Identity Center — revoke the user's assignments and they lose access across all accounts immediately.

Option A (IAM users everywhere) = credential sprawl, hard to deprovision, no SSO. Option C is a catastrophic root-credential violation. Option D shares credentials — anti-pattern. The trap: candidates default to 'IAM users' because that's the primitive they know; Identity Center is the modern answer for human workforce access.
questions.pattern: Workforce access pattern: IAM Identity Center + corporate IdP for employees; Identity Center internal store or separate IdP for contractors/vendors. Permission sets = reusable role templates assigned to users/groups per account. Audit via CloudTrail. Deprovision = single action. For break-glass: dedicated IAM emergency-access users in protected accounts, rarely used, MFA + key custody.

questions.scenario: A company wants to encrypt EBS volumes attached to EC2 instances with a customer-managed key, and ensure that any snapshot created from these volumes is ALSO encrypted with the same key automatically.
questions.question: Which statement is CORRECT about EBS encryption?
choices: EBS encryption must be manually enabled on each snapshot; it is not inherited from the source volume

Snapshots of encrypted EBS volumes are automatically encrypted with the same KMS key, and any volume restored from those snapshots is encrypted; encryption can be enabled as a default in the Region via EC2 settings
EBS volumes cannot be encrypted with customer-managed KMS keys — only AWS-managed keys
Encryption applies only to io1/io2 volume types, not gp3 or standard
questions.explanation:
EBS encryption behavior: snapshots of encrypted volumes are automatically encrypted (same KMS key). Volumes restored from encrypted snapshots are encrypted. Copies of encrypted snapshots can be re-encrypted with a different KMS key (useful for cross-region/cross-account). EBS encryption by default can be enabled per Region in EC2 Account Settings — any new volume in that region is encrypted automatically. All volume types support encryption (gp2, gp3, io1, io2, st1, sc1).

Option A is wrong — snapshot encryption is inherited. Option C is wrong — EBS supports customer-managed KMS keys. Option D is wrong — encryption works for all EBS types. The trap: candidates who haven't touched EBS encryption may think encryption is opt-in per snapshot or limited to certain volume types.
questions.pattern: EBS encryption rules: customer-managed or AWS-managed KMS keys supported, inheritance through snapshot → volume chain, re-encryption during snapshot copy to change key. 'Encryption by default' regional setting forces all new volumes encrypted. No measurable performance cost. Same for EFS (KMS keys), RDS (must enable at creation, not retroactive — workaround is snapshot + restore-with-encryption), S3 (SSE-S3/KMS/C), DynamoDB (encrypted at rest by default with AWS-owned key).
